elements.sent-2411-002: However, the flexibility that marks the potential of platform trials also creates inferential challenges.
elements.sent-2411-003: Two key challenges are the precise definition of treatment effects and the robust and efficient inference on these effects.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address these challenges, we first define a clinically meaningful estimand that characterizes the treatment effect as a function of the expected outcomes under two given treatments among concurrently eligible patients.
elements.sent-2411-005: Then, we develop weighting and post-stratification methods for estimation of treatment effects with minimal assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-006: To fully leverage the efficiency potential of data from concurrently eligible patients, we also consider a model-assisted approach for baseline covariate adjustment to gain efficiency while maintaining robustness against model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-007: We derive and compare asymptotic distributions of proposed estimators in theory and propose robust variance estimators.
elements.sent-2411-008: The proposed estimators are empirically evaluated in a simulation study and illustrated using the SIMPLIFY trial.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our methods are implemented in the R package RobinCID.
elements.sent-2411-010: The traditional paradigm of conducting a separate clinical trial for every investigational treatment has become ever more expensive and challenging \citep{saville2016efficiencies, woodcock2017master .}
elements.sent-2411-011: As a result, the landscape of clinical trials has been evolving, to make clinical trials faster and more efficient while continuing to provide reliable information on safety and effectiveness \citep{ostp .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The platform trial has emerged as an innovative trial design to expedite the evaluation of potentially effective treatments \citep{berry2015platform, saville2016efficiencies, woodcock2017master, park2020overview, gold2022platform, burki2023platform, pitre2023methodology .}
elements.sent-2411-013: It is a dynamic clinical trial that uses a master protocol (i.e., one overarching protocol) to evaluate multiple treatments in an ongoing manner, where new treatments can be added to the platform as they become available, and existing ones can leave the platform based on a finding of futility or efficacy.
elements.sent-2411-014: Such a trial structure not only streamlines operations but also has the potential to improve statistical efficiency by facilitating the sharing of data between different treatment evaluations \citep{mehta2023role .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The effectiveness of platform trials in addressing urgent medical challenges quickly is evidenced by the hallmark RECOVERY platform trial, which was designed to rapidly evaluate COVID-19 therapies during the pandemic \citep{normand2021recovery,buenconsejo2023platform .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Platform trials are also attractive in the exploratory phase to quickly screen multiple treatments for diseases \citep{wang2019spy and in rare diseases to enhance the chance of identifying effective treatments \citep{dhaenens2024platform .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Two examples of platform trials are presented in Section 2 to provide detailed illustrations.
elements.sent-2411-018: Although platform trials offer several advantages over traditional trials, they also create challenges in statistical inference on treatment effects, as highlighted in a recent Food and Drug Administration draft guidance \citep{fda:2023platform .}
elements.sent-2411-019: One primary challenge is how to define a meaningful treatment effect in a platform trial setting, as multiple treatments may target different biomarker-defined subgroups and enter the trial at different times --- a complexity that does not exist in traditional trials.
elements.sent-2411-020: In platform trials, when comparing a control and a treatment of interest, many researchers \citep{lee2020including, dodd2021platform and \cite{fda:2023platform have recommended using eligible and concurrent controls, which refer to the patients who are eligible to receive the treatment and are concurrently randomized to the control arm during the same enrollment period as the other patients being assigned to the treatment arm.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The use of eligible and concurrent controls, or concurrently eligible patients, preserves the integrity of randomized comparisons and inference on treatment effects.
elements.sent-2411-022: In contrast, patients who are either ineligible or nonconcurrent may not be comparable to the treated group due to measured and unmeasured differences \citep{fda:2023platform .}
elements.sent-2411-023: In Section \ref{sec: setup , we explicitly present, for the first time in the literature, how to construct a clinically meaningful estimand with clear specificity on the population of concurrently eligible patients, which aligns with the \cite{ICHE9-R1 estimand framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The next challenge is on robust estimation of the defined treatment effects for concurrently eligible patients.
elements.sent-2411-025: Given the adaptive nature of platform trials, where treatments can be added and removed, the available treatment options vary over time.
elements.sent-2411-026: This dynamic, coupled with different eligibility criteria for each treatment, means that the probabilities of treatment assignments can vary over time and patient characteristics --- another complexity absent in traditional trials.
elements.sent-2411-027: Thus, naively comparing outcome means between two treatment arms may yield biased results, an example of Simpson’s paradox \citep{collignon2020current,park2020overview,dodd2021platform, lee2021statistical,meyer2021systematic .}
elements.sent-2411-028: The literature on statistical methods for platform trials is expanding, though it primarily focuses on model-based techniques for incorporating nonconcurrent controls, which can be sensitive to model misspecification \citep{saville_bayesian_2022,roig2022model, elm_flexible_2012, wang_bayesian_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Some research has been conducted on statistical methods for concurrently eligible patients.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, there remains a lack of rigorous investigation on how to make robust inference on treatment effects in platform trials under the same minimal statistical assumptions as those in traditional clinical trials.
elements.sent-2411-031: To fill this gap, in Section 4, we develop methods for estimating treatment effects for concurrently eligible patients using inverse probability weighting or post-stratification to account for varying treatment assignment probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-032: Additionally, we consider model-assisted approaches for covariate adjustment to gain efficiency while maintaining robustness against model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-033: In Section 5, we theoretically establish the asymptotic distributions of proposed estimators and compare their asymptotic relative efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-034: We provide robust variance estimators in Section 6.
elements.sent-2411-035: Simulation results and an application of our methods to the SIMPLIFY trial \citep{mayer2023discontinuation are in Section \ref{sec: sim .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: First, we present a stylistic example of a platform trial in Figure \ref{fig: schema to provide a concrete illustration of its key features.}
elements.sent-2411-037: This trial comprises five sub-studies, each containing a randomized controlled comparison of several treatments.
elements.sent-2411-038: Initiating or stopping a sub-study is governed by a master protocol.
elements.sent-2411-039: Additionally, each sub-study has an intervention-specific appendix outlining protocol elements specific to that sub-study.
elements.sent-2411-040: Patients are randomized first to eligible sub-studies and subsequently to treatment arms within a sub-study, which gives explicit treatment allocation probabilities for every subject, sufficient for the identification of treatment effects and statistical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-041: Real trials resembling this structure include Lung-MAP for lung cancer \citep{herbst2015lung,redman2020biomarker and an ongoing trial for chronic pain (ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT05986292).}
elements.figure-2411-001: \spacingset{1.2} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Platform_Trial_Schema.png} \caption{A stylistic example of a platform trial. The x-axis represents calendar time discretized into windows with different potential enrollment in components of the platform trial. The figure illustrates both different treatment comparisons and different patient subgroups evolving over time.} \label{fig: schema}
elements.sent-2411-042: In Figure \ref{fig: schema , each box represents a sub-study along with its status, and each striped block within a box represents the enrollment period for a treatment evaluated in that sub-study.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The horizontal axis denotes calendar time.
elements.sent-2411-044: Enrollment windows are defined by changes in the available treatments for enrollment.
elements.sent-2411-045: For instance, the initiation of enrollment for sub-study 3 marks the start of enrollment window 2, within which treatment 4 is compared to treatment 1 as the control among patients with disease subtype A.
elements.sent-2411-046: Likewise, the conclusion of the enrollment period for sub-study 2 defines the start of enrollment period 3.
elements.sent-2411-047: Subsequently, the start of treatment 3 as the new control in place of treatment 1 among subtype A defines the start of enrollment window 4.
elements.sent-2411-048: The following is a narrative of Figure \ref{fig: schema according to the order of enrollment windows.}
elements.sent-2411-049: In this example, there are two initial sub-studies.
elements.sent-2411-050: Patients with any disease subtype is eligible to sub-study 1, while sub-study 2 is restricted to patients with subtype A.
elements.sent-2411-051: During enrollment window 1, patients with subtype A are randomly assigned to sub-study 1 or 2, and then randomized to treatment 1 or 2 if they are in sub-study 1 and to treatment 1 or 3 if they are in sub-study 2.
elements.sent-2411-052: Patients with disease subtypes other than A are enrolled in sub-study 1 and then randomized to treatment 1 or 2.
elements.sent-2411-053: Sub-study 3 is added to the platform trial when a new intervention treatment 4 becomes available for disease subtype A.
elements.sent-2411-054: Following this addition, a patient with subtype A is randomly assigned to either sub-study 1, 2, or 3, and subsequently randomized to the treatments being assessed within the respective sub-study.
elements.sent-2411-055: After sub-study 2 reaches full enrollment, it closes its enrollment.
elements.sent-2411-056: Later during this period, treatment 3 meets the pre-specified success criteria.
elements.sent-2411-057: Since treatment 3 has been demonstrated to be superior to treatment 1 for subtype A, it replaces treatment 1 as the control for subtype A.
elements.sent-2411-058: Consequently, treatment 3 replaces treatment 1 in sub-study 3.
elements.sent-2411-059: Moreover, sub-study 1 excludes patients with subtype A, and sub-study 4 is introduced to continue evaluating treatment 2 in comparison to the new control treatment 3 for patients with subtype A.
elements.sent-2411-060: Sub-study 5 is added upon the introduction of a new treatment 5 for patients positive for biomarker B.
elements.sent-2411-061: In sub-study 5, treatment 5 is evaluated against treatment 1 for patients without subtype A, or to treatment 3 for patients with subtype A.
elements.sent-2411-062: Sub-study 4 has finished its planned enrollment and is no longer accepting new patients.
elements.sent-2411-063: A new treatment, treatment 6, has been introduced in sub-study 5 for patients positive for biomarker B.
elements.sent-2411-064: During this enrollment window, treatment 2 in sub-study 4 fails to meet the pre-specified success criteria.
elements.sent-2411-065: As the platform trial continues, additional sub-studies or treatments may be added as new interventions emerge for one or more targeted groups of patients, while existing sub-studies may be concluded due to either meeting success criteria or demonstrating futility.
elements.sent-2411-066: As exemplified in Figure \ref{fig: schema , platform trials can be very flexible: adding new sub-studies and/or treatments with different eligibility, dropping treatments or sub-studies, and changing the control in the middle of the trial.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Moreover, compared to a traditional clinical trial, it enables potential pooling of data across sub-studies.
elements.sent-2411-068: Specifically, in traditional clinical trials, patients self-select into studies and are randomized into treatment arms, with treatment comparisons conducted solely within each study.
elements.sent-2411-069: In contrast, platform trials enroll patients into a master protocol, randomizing them into eligible sub-studies and subsequently treatment arms, and consequently, patients can be pooled across sub-studies.
elements.sent-2411-070: For example, when comparing treatments 1 and 2, in a platform trial like Figure \ref{fig: schema , patients from not only sub-study 1 but also other sub-studies receiving treatment 1 can be included in the analysis, whereas we analyze data within sub-study 1 in a traditional trial.}
elements.sent-2411-071: See the simulation results and discussions in Section 7.1 for the efficiency gain.
elements.subsection-2411-002: A real example: the SIMPLIFY trial
elements.sent-2411-072: Our second example is a real platform design aliased SIMPLIFY for people with cystic fibrosis \citep{mayer2023discontinuation .}
elements.sent-2411-073: It contains two concurrently run multi-center randomized controlled studies at 80 participating clinics across the United States in the Cystic Fibrosis Therapeutics Development Network.
elements.sent-2411-074: The primary objective for each study is to determine whether discontinuing either hypertonic saline (HS) or dornase alfa (DA), respectively, is non-inferior to continuing therapy in individuals using the highly effective modulator, elexacaftor plus tezacaftor plus ivacaftor (ETI).
elements.sent-2411-075: The outcome is the 6-week mean absolute change in percent-predicted forced expiratory volume in one second (ppFEV$_1$).
elements.sent-2411-076: Figure \ref{fig: simplify depicts the SIMPLIFY study design.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \spacingset{1.2} \includegraphics[scale=.7]{SIMPLIFY.pdf} \caption{\label{fig: simplify} SIMPLIFY study design. All enrolled individuals have initiated ETI for at least 90 days and belong to one of three groups: those receiving HS only, those receiving DA only, and those receiving both therapies. Individuals on a single therapy enter the corresponding study, while those on both are randomized to the HS or DA study, first with a 1:1 probability and then with a 3:1 probability during subsequent enrollment. All individuals in HS study (or DA study) are randomized 1:1 to continue or discontinue HS (or DA).}
elements.sent-2411-077: Consider a platform trial with $J$ treatments and $n$ patients.
elements.sent-2411-078: For each patient $i$, let $\bmW_i$ be a vector of all observed baseline variables (e.g., baseline covariates and enrollment window by calendar time) not affected by the treatment, $A_i$ be the treatment assignment indicator that equals $j$ if the patient is assigned to arm $j$, $Y_i^{(j)}$ be the potential outcome under treatment $j $, $j=1,...,J$ \citep{Neyman:1923a,Rubin:1974 , and $Y_i=Y_i^{(A_i)}$ be the observed outcome.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Throughout, $(\bmW_i, Y_i^{(1) , \dots, Y_i^{(J) , A_i)$, $i=1,\dots, n$, are independent and identically distributed realizations of $(\bmW, Y^{(1) , \dots, Y^{(J) , A)$ with finite second-order moments.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The following assumption describes the randomization procedure in platform trials.
elements.sent-2411-081: In traditional trials, the randomization probability is typically a fixed positive constant.
elements.sent-2411-082: However, in platform trials, this probability, $\pi_j(Z)$, varies depending on a baseline discrete variable $Z$ and may be zero for some treatments.
elements.sent-2411-083: Notably, $Z$ usually includes the enrollment window by calendar time and some key patient characteristics, as the enrollment window determines which treatments are available for enrollment, and patient characteristics determine the treatments for which a patient is eligible.
elements.sent-2411-084: Hence, $\pi_j(Z)$ may be zero if treatment $j$ has not entered the platform trial or has closed its enrollment, or if the patient does not meet the eligibility requirements for treatment $j$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Additionally, non-zero values of $\pi_j(Z)$ can differ over time and patient characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-086: Values of $\pi_j(\bmZ)$, $j=1,...,J$, are known by design, but $\pi_{j (\bmZ)$ should be viewed as random due to the randomness of $\bmZ$.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The example in Figure \ref{fig: schema illustrates these dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Here, {$Z=(Z_{\rm win , Z_{\rm sub , Z_{\rm B+ )$ represent the enrollment window by calendar time, disease subtype (A or not A), and biomarker B (positive or negative), respectively, where $Z_{\rm B+}$ does not affect treatment assignment until enrollment window 5.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: For simplicity, consider randomization with equal probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-090: If patient $i$ with subtype A enters the trial during enrollment window 1 in which only sub-studies 1 and 2 are open, this patient is randomized to either sub-study with equal probability.
elements.sent-2411-091: If randomized to sub-study 1, the patient is further randomized to treatment 1 or 2 with equal probability.
elements.sent-2411-092: If randomized to sub-study 2, the patient is further randomized to treatment 1 or 3 with equal probability.
elements.sent-2411-093: Consequently, for this patient $i$, $\pi_{1 (\bmZ_i)= 0.5\times 0.5+0.5\times 0.5= 0.5$, $\pi_{2 (\bmZ_i) = 0.5\times 0.5=0.25$, $\pi_{3 (\bmZ_i) = 0.5\times 0.5=0.25$, with all other $\pi_{j (\bmZ_i)$ values equal to 0.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: If another patient $i'$ without subtype A enters the trial during the same enrollment window 1, then this patient is assigned to sub-study 1 with probability 1.
elements.sent-2411-095: Consequently, $\pi_{1 (\bmZ_{i' )=\pi_{2 (\bmZ_{i' )= 0.5$ and all other $\pi_{j (\bmZ_{i' )$ values are 0.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Similarly, if another patient $i{''}$ with subtype A enters the trial during enrollment window 2 in which sub-studies 1,2,3 are open, this patient has $\pi_1(Z_{i'' )= 0.5$, $\pi_2(Z_{i'' )= \pi_3(Z_{i'' )=\pi_4(Z_{i'' )= 1/6$, with all other $\pi_{j (\bmZ_{i'' )$ values equal to 0.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: These cases exemplify how the positivity and values of $\pi_j(Z)$ can vary with $Z$.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Treatment effects for concurrently eligible patients
elements.sent-2411-098: We first define a clinically meaningful estimand \citep{ICHE9-R1 for comparing a given pair of treatments, $j$ and $k$, one of which is typically a control.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Formally, the concurrently eligible patients for treatments $j$ and $k$ are those with $\pi_j(\bmZ) >0 $ and $ \pi_k(\bmZ)>0 $.
elements.sent-2411-100: We provide two examples to illustrate this concept.
elements.sent-2411-101: The first example is based on Figure \ref{fig: schema .}
elements.sent-2411-102: When an investigator compares treatments 1 and 3 at the end of sub-study 2, the concurrently eligible patients are all subtype A patients from enrollment windows 1 and 2, which includes all patients in sub-study 2, as well as patients with subtype A in sub-study 1 and all patients in sub-study 3 from the first two windows.
elements.sent-2411-103: Patients without subtype A or enrolled in later windows are not included, as they are either ineligible or nonconcurrent.
elements.sent-2411-104: The second example is based on the SIMPLIFY design in Figure \ref{fig: simplify .}
elements.sent-2411-105: The original analysis by \cite{mayer2023discontinuation calculated the effects of discontinuing vs. continuing HS or DA within each study separately.}
elements.sent-2411-106: However, this approach did not utilize all concurrently eligible patients with additional control data that could have been used from participants assigned to continue therapy across either study.
elements.sent-2411-107: Thus, when comparing discontinuing HS versus continuing therapy, for instance, the concurrently eligible patients are all individuals on HS regardless of their DA status, which includes all participants in the HS study, as well as those on both HS and DA in the DA study.
elements.sent-2411-108: We propose the following estimand to define the treatment effect for two given treatments $j$ and $k$ among concurrently eligible patients: \begin{equation}{\bm \vartheta}_{jk} = \left( \begin{array}{c} \theta_{jk} \theta_{kj} \end{array}\right) = \left( \begin{array}{c} E ( Y^{(j)} \mid \pi_{j}(\bmZ) >0, \pi_{k}(\bmZ)>0 ) E ( Y^{(k)} \mid \pi_{j}(\bmZ)>0, \pi_{k}(\bmZ)>0 ) \end{array}\right) , \label{effect1}\end{equation} where both subscripts $j$ and $k$ are used in $\bm\vartheta_{jk}$, $\theta_{jk}$, and $\theta_{kj}$ because both $\pi_j(\bmZ)$ and $\pi_k(\bmZ)$ are involved.
elements.sent-2411-109: Time can represent many background factors that may be changing although this is not explicitly recognized in estimand specification for traditional clinical trials.
elements.sent-2411-110: Our defined population of concurrently eligible patients is the largest population that can preserve the integrity of randomization when inferring treatment effects.
elements.sent-2411-111: Individuals outside this group have zero probability of being assigned to treatments $j$ or $k$, or both, due to their ineligibility or the unavailability of treatments, thereby necessitating extrapolation.
elements.sent-2411-112: This is the reason why we do not consider the population means $E(Y^{(j) )$ and $E(Y^{(k) )$ that are defined for the entire platform trial population.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Conversely, our estimand in \eqref{effect1 represents the treatment effect for a broader population compared to just those enrolled in a specific sub-study.}
elements.sent-2411-114: Our estimand in \eqref{effect1 truly respects the eligibility criteria for the given pair of treatments for comparison, rather than on the subset assigned to a particular sub-study.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Throughout the paper, we use the notation convention that $a_{kj}$ is defined by swapping subscripts $j$ and $k$ in $a_{jk}$, e.g., $\theta_{jk}$ and $\theta_{kj}$ defined in (\ref{effect1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-116: Based on the estimand ${\bm \vartheta _{jk}$ in (\ref{effect1 ), the treatment effect of $j$ versus $k$ can be defined as the linear contrast $ \theta_{jk - \theta_{kj}$, the ratio $ \theta_{jk / \theta_{kj}$, or some other function of ${\bm \vartheta _{jk}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: Our approach to defining treatment effects is adaptable to situations where population characteristics other than means are considered, or when the treatment effect for a targeted subgroup is of primary interest.
elements.sent-2411-118: After we define the estimand ${\bm \vartheta _{jk}$ in (\ref{effect1 ), the next challenge is estimating each component of ${\bm \vartheta _{jk}$ in (\ref{effect1 ), because even among the concurrently eligible patients defined by $\pi_{j (\bmZ)>0$ and $ \pi_{k (\bmZ)>0 $, these non-zero probabilities $\pi_{j (\bmZ), \pi_{k (\bmZ)$ may still vary over time and patient characteristics, leading to confounding.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: A naive method is to estimate the component $\theta_{jk $ by the sample mean $\bar{Y _{jk}$ of $Y_i$'s from patients with $\pi_j(\bmZ_i)>0$, $\pi_k(\bmZ_i)>0$, and $A_i=j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: While $\bar{Y _{jk}$ is valid in traditional trials, it is typically biased under platform trials as it overlooks confounding by $\bmZ$, i.e., the fact that $Z$ affects both treatment and outcome.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Specifically, as shown in the Supplementary Material, as $n \to \infty$, \begin{equation}\bar{Y}_{jk} \to \frac{E\{ \pi_j(\bmZ) E( Y^{(j)} \mid \bmZ) \mid \pi_j(\bmZ)>0, \pi_k(\bmZ)>0 \}}{ E\{ \pi_j(\bmZ)\mid \pi_j(\bmZ)>0, \pi_k(\bmZ)>0\}} \quad in probability . \label{effect2}\end{equation} The limit of $\bar{Y _{jk}$ in (\ref{effect2 ) is not equal to $\theta_{jk}$ in (\ref{effect1 ) unless either $\pi_j(\bmZ)$ does not vary with $\bmZ$ or $Y^{(j) \perp \bmZ$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Take the trial described in Section 2.1 and Figure \ref{fig: schema as an example, where $\pi_j(\bmZ)$ varies with $\bmZ$, the condition $Y^{(j) \perp \bmZ$ unrealistically rules out the influence of disease subtype on the outcome, as well as potential temporal effects, such as later recruited patients being healthier than those recruited earlier.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: The problem with the naive estimator $\bar{Y _{jk}$ arises from confounding by $\pi_j(\bmZ)$, not from conditioning on $\pi_j(\bmZ)>0$ and $\pi_k(\bmZ)>0$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: In traditional clinical trials, $\bar{Y _{jk}$ is valid because $\pi_j(\bmZ)$ does not vary with $\bmZ$.}
elements.sent-2411-125: As noted in Section \ref{subsec: naive method , the naive method is biased because it fails to account for confounding by $Z$.}
elements.sent-2411-126: Under Assumption \ref{assump: randomization , since the true probability of receiving treatment $\pi_j(Z)$ is known, this confounding can be addressed using inverse probability weighting (IPW).}
elements.sent-2411-127: The identification formula is (the proof is in the Supplementary Material): \begin{equation}\theta_{jk} = E\bigg\{ \frac{I(A= j) Y }{\pi_{j}(\bmZ) } \ \Big| \ \pi_{j}(\bmZ)>0, \pi_{k}(\bmZ)>0\bigg\} , \label{eq: IPW identification}\end{equation} where $I( \cdot )$ denotes the indicator function.
elements.sent-2411-128: Formula (\ref{eq: IPW identification ) naturally suggests the following IPW estimator of $\theta_{jk}$, which is widely used in survey sampling \citep{horvitz1952generalization and causal inference \citep{rosenbaum1983central , \begin{equation}\ipw = \frac{1}{n_{jk}} \sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \frac{ I(A_i=j)Y_i }{\pi_{j}(\bmZ_i)},\end{equation} where ${{{\cal I _{jk = \{ i: \pi_j(\bmZ_i) >0 , \pi_k(\bmZ_i)>0 \} $ and $n_{jk}$ is the number of elements in ${{{\cal I _{jk}$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: This IPW estimator may be improved by the following stabilized IPW (SIPW) \citep{hajek1971comment,robins2000marginal estimator, which normalizes the weights $I(A_i=j) / \pi_j(\bmZ_i)$ for $ i \in {{{\cal I _{jk}$ to sum to 1: \begin{equation}\sipw = \bigg\{ \sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \frac{ I(A_i=j) }{\pi_j(\bmZ_i)}\bigg\}^{-1} \sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \frac{ I(A_i=j) Y_i }{\pi_j(\bmZ_i)} .\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: Both the IPW and SIPW estimators are asymptotically normal (Theorem 1 of Section 5).
elements.sent-2411-131: The IPW and SIPW methods utilize some covariate information through weighting with $\pi_j(\bmZ)$.
elements.sent-2411-132: To improve efficiency, we can adjust for $\bmZ$ and other baseline covariates using a model-assisted approach \citep{Tsiatis:2008aa, ye2021better, bannick2023general , which incorporates these covariates through a working model between the outcome and covariates and produces an asymptotically unbiased estimator even if the working model is incorrect.}
elements.sent-2411-133: This robustness makes model-assisted approaches increasingly popular and recommended by the regulatory agencies \citep{ICHE9,ema:2015aa,fda:2019aa .}
elements.sent-2411-134: Specifically, let $\bmX \subset \bmW$ denote the observed vector of baseline covariates for adjustment, which may contain or overlap with $\bmZ$.
elements.sent-2411-135: We estimate $ E(Y^{(j) \mid \bm X, \pi_j(\bmZ)>0, \pi_k(\bmZ)>0, A= j) $ by $\hat{\mu _{jk (\bmX)$, using a potentially misspecified working model.}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: For continuous outcomes, $ \hat \mu_{jk (\bmX) $ is usually from least squares regression of $Y_i$ on $\bmX$ using data where $\pi_j(\bmZ)>0, \pi_k(\bmZ)>0$ and $ A= j$.}
elements.sent-2411-137: For binary outcomes, $\hat \mu_{jk (\bmX) $ is often obtained from a logistic working model.}
elements.sent-2411-138: After $\hat{\mu _{jk (\cdot) $ is obtained, we can adjust for covariate $\bm X$ using the augmented inverse probability weighting (AIPW) \citep{robins1994estimation , resulting in the following AIPW estimator of $\theta_{jk}$, \begin{equation}\aipw = \ \frac{1}{n_{jk}} \sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \frac{ I(A_i=j) \{Y_i - \hat \mu_{jk}(\bmX_i) \} }{\pi_j(\bmZ_i)} + \frac{1}{n_{jk}} \sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \hat \mu_{jk}(\bmX_i) .\end{equation} Interestingly, in its last term, the AIPW estimator leverages covariate information from all concurrently eligible patients in ${{{\cal I _{jk =\{i: \pi_j(\bmZ_i)>0, \pi_k(\bmZ_i)>0\}$, including those not receiving treatments $j$ or $k$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: For example, in Figure \ref{fig: schema , when estimating the effect of treatment 3 versus treatment 1 at the end of enrollment window 2 using AIPW, the estimator also incorporates covariate data from subtype A patients assigned to treatment 2 (in sub-study 1) and treatment 4 (in sub-study 3) during enrollment windows 1 and 2.}
elements.sent-2411-140: These covariate data are not used in the IPW and SIPW estimators.
elements.sent-2411-141: Thus, AIPW offers a robust method to pool data from all concurrently eligible patients, thereby further extracting the potential of efficiency gain through platform trials.
elements.sent-2411-142: Similarly, one can use the stabilized AIPW (SAIPW) estimator by normalizing the weights $I(A_i=j) / \pi_j(\bmZ_i)$ for $ i \in {{{\cal I _{jk}$, resulting in the following SAIPW estimator of $\theta_{jk}$, \begin{equation}\saipw = \bigg\{ \sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \frac{ I(A_i=j) }{\pi_j(\bmZ_i)}\bigg\}^{-1} \sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \frac{ I(A_i=j) \{ Y_i - \hat \mu_{jk} (\bmX_i)\} }{\pi_j(\bmZ_i)} + \frac{1}{n_{jk}} \sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \hat \mu_{jk}(\bmX_i).\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-145: An alternative method to account for the varying $\pi_j(\bmZ)$ is to stratify subjects based on the values that $\pi_j(\bmZ)$ can take.

elements.figure-2411-007: \spacingset{1.2} \includegraphics[width=.9\textwidth]{SIMPLIFY_result_fig_updated.png} \caption{Estimates and 95\% confidence intervals (in \%) in the SIMPLIFY trial} \label{fig:enter-label}
elements.sent-2411-263: Platform trials offer the potential to enhance statistical efficiency by facilitating the sharing of data between different treatment evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-264: However, realizing this potential requires careful consideration of several challenges, such as defining meaningful estimands that reflect eligibility and concurrency, and developing robust estimation techniques that accommodate the varying probabilities of treatment assignments over time and differing patient characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-265: In this article, we explicitly present how to construct a clinically meaningful estimand with clear specificity on the population of concurrently eligible patients.
elements.sent-2411-266: To estimate and infer this estimand with minimal statistical assumptions, we develop methods using weighting or post-stratification to account for varying treatment assignment probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-267: We also consider model-assisted approaches for covariate adjustment.
elements.sent-2411-268: We provide explicit formulas for robust variance estimation, which have been implemented in our R package, RobinCID.
elements.sent-2411-269: Through theoretical analysis and simulation studies assessing the relative efficiency of these methods, we find that the stabilized IPW (SIPW) and post-stratification (PS) significantly enhance the efficiency of the IPW estimator.
elements.sent-2411-270: Additionally, model-assisted approaches for covariate adjustment (SAIPW or APS) generally improve efficiency while maintaining robustness against model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-271: A detailed relative efficiency comparison is in Figure \ref{fig:eff .}
elements.sent-2411-272: Note that although SIPW is less efficient than PS asymptotically, it is still useful when there are sparse strata because SIPW may have better finite-sample performance and there may be situations where the PS estimator or its variance estimator cannot be computed.. \spacingset{0.94 \bibliographystyle{apalike.}}
elements.sent-2411-273: However, its flexibility introduces inferential challenges, with two fundamental ones being the precise definition of treatment effects and robust, efficient inference on these effects.
elements.sent-2411-274: Central to these challenges is the definition of an appropriate target population for the estimand, as some commonly used populations can be unexpectedly problematic.
elements.sent-2411-275: This article, for the first time, presents a clear framework for constructing a clinically meaningful estimand with precise specificity regarding the population of interest.
elements.sent-2411-276: The proposed estimand defines the treatment effect as a contrast of expected outcomes between two treatments within the entire concurrently eligible (ECE) population — the largest population that preserves the integrity of randomization — establishing a foundation for future research in platform trials.
elements.sent-2411-277: To fully leverage the efficiency potential of platform trials, we also consider a model-assisted approach for baseline covariate adjustment to gain efficiency while maintaining robustness against model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-278: The proposed estimators are empirically evaluated in a simulation study and illustrated in the SIMPLIFY trial, using the R package RobinCID.
elements.sent-2411-279: Two examples are in Section 2 to provide concrete illustrations.
elements.sent-2411-280: While platform trials offer several advantages over traditional trials, they also pose challenges for statistical inference on treatment effects, as highlighted in a recent Food and Drug Administration draft guidance \citep{fda:2023platform .}
elements.sent-2411-281: Despite the growing number of platform trials being conducted or completed, and the expanding body of statistical literature on the topic, two fundamental questions remain unaddressed.. 1.
elements.sent-2411-282: How to define a clinically meaningful estimand? An estimand is ``a precise description of the treatment effect reflecting the clinical question posed by a given clinical trials objective'' and is of ultimate importance \citep{ICHE9-R1 .}
elements.sent-2411-283: In platform trials, defining an estimand is challenging because multiple treatments may target different biomarker-defined subgroups of individuals and enter the trial at different times -- complexities not present in traditional trials.
elements.sent-2411-284: Of the five estimand attributes in \cite{ICHE9-R1 , special care is needed when defining the population of interest.}
elements.sent-2411-285: Many researchers \citep{lee2020including, dodd2021platform and \cite{fda:2023platform have recommended using a population of individuals who are concurrently randomized and meet the eligibility criteria, as this preserves the integrity of randomized comparisons and avoids systematic differences between treatment groups due to measured and unmeasured factors.}}
elements.sent-2411-286: However, this general recommendation does not uniquely define the population, which can lead to ambiguity and confusion.
elements.sent-2411-287: For instance, in Example 1 in Section 3.2, a seemingly reasonable population (and thus the estimand) may depend arbitrarily on randomization probabilities, even though it technically follows the general recommendation in \cite{fda:2023platform .}
elements.sent-2411-288: To address this question, in Section 3.2, we define a population for comparing two treatments (typically with one as a control) that not only follows the general recommendations in \cite{fda:2023platform but also respects the eligibility criteria while avoiding arbitrary dependencies on randomization probabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-289: We refer to this as the entire concurrently eligible (ECE) population, which is the largest population that preserves the integrity of randomized comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-290: Using Example 1 in Section 3.2, we explain why this population is sensible by drawing simple connections to traditional parallel-group trials.
elements.sent-2411-291: The corresponding estimand for the ECE population is defined in Section 3.3.
elements.sent-2411-292: Additionally, if a different target population is of interest, the ECE population can be re-weighted to resemble the target population.
elements.sent-2411-293: For the first time in the literature, our proposal shows how to construct a clinically meaningful estimand with clear specificity on the population of interest.
elements.sent-2411-294: Given the central importance of the estimand, our contribution enhances the fundamental understanding of platform trials and lays the groundwork for future statistical research, including both frequentist and Bayesian analyses, as well as topics such as interim analysis and the use of nonconcurrent controls.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-295: How to robustly estimate and infer the defined treatment effect? The next challenge is estimating the defined estimand for the treatment effect.
elements.sent-2411-296: This dynamic, coupled with different eligibility criteria for each treatment, means that the probabilities of treatment assignments can vary over time and individual characteristics --- another complexity absent in traditional trials.
elements.sent-2411-297: To fill this gap, we develop methods for estimating the target estimand using inverse probability weighting (IPW) or post-stratification to account for varying treatment assignment probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-298: We also consider model-assisted approaches for covariate adjustment to gain efficiency while maintaining robustness against model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-299: To guide practitioners in selecting these methods, we derive the asymptotic variance and asymptotic relative efficiency of these estimation methods.
elements.sent-2411-300: These results are new and have not appeared in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-301: The increasing popularity of platform trials has spurred a surge of research addressing their statistical challenges in both design and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-302: Many studies have focused on adaptive designs, which aim to efficiently screen candidate treatments and adaptively randomize individuals to the most promising treatments \citep{yuan2016midas,kaizer2018multi, ventz2018adding .}
elements.sent-2411-303: Additionally, \cite{bofill2024optimal examined the optimal allocation ratios in these trials.}
elements.sent-2411-304: A key concern in the analysis of platform trials is the potential for time trends, which can introduce bias when using nonconcurrent controls \citep{bofill2023use .}
elements.sent-2411-305: To address this, various strategies have been proposed to account for time trends in the analysis \citep{elm_flexible_2012, saville_bayesian_2022,roig2022model, jiang2023nonconcurrent, wang_bayesian_2023, guo2024treatment .}
elements.sent-2411-306: However, all these statistical methods have predominantly relied on correct modeling and are sensitive to model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-307: The literature on non-model-based approaches for concurrently eligible individuals is very limited.
elements.sent-2411-308: There has been no rigorous investigation into defining a clinically meaningful estimand in a general framework, independent of specific studies or modeling assumptions, or into robustly estimating and inferring treatment effects in platform trials under the same minimal statistical assumptions used in traditional clinical trials.
elements.sent-2411-309: Section 2 shows a stylized example and a real platform trial design.
elements.sent-2411-310: Section 3 introduces and discusses the proposed ECE population and corresponding estimand.
elements.sent-2411-311: In Section 4, we develop robust estimation methods, without or with covariate adjustment.
elements.sent-2411-312: Section 5 establishes the asymptotic distributions and relative efficiency of the proposed estimators.
elements.sent-2411-313: In Section 6, we provide robust variance estimators.
elements.sent-2411-314: Simulation results, along with an application of our methods to the SIMPLIFY trial \citep{mayer2023discontinuation , are presented in Section \ref{sec: sim .}}
elements.sent-2411-315: We conclude with a summary and recommendations in Section \ref{sec: discussion .}
elements.sent-2411-316: First, we present a stylistic example of platform trials in two forms.
elements.sent-2411-317: The first is a sub-study platform trial, illustrated in Figure \ref{fig: schema (a), which evaluates treatments in separate sub-studies.}
elements.sent-2411-318: The second is a multi-arm platform trial, shown in Figure \ref{fig: schema (b), which evaluates multiple treatments without dividing the platform trial into sub-studies.}
elements.figure-2411-008: \spacingset{1.2} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/sub-study.pdf} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/multi-arm.pdf} \caption{A stylistic example of two forms: (a) sub-study platform trials, (b) multi-arm platform trials. Each box in (a) represents a sub-study and its status. Each striped block within a box in (a) or standalone in (b) represents the enrollment period for a treatment evaluated. The horizontal axis shows calendar time, divided into discrete windows based on changes in available treatments for enrollment, such as the initiation or conclusion of a sub-study or treatment enrollment period.} \label{fig: schema}
elements.sent-2411-319: The sub-study platform trial in Figure \ref{fig: schema (a) comprises four sub-studies, each containing a randomized controlled comparison of two treatments.}
elements.sent-2411-320: Individuals are randomized first to eligible sub-studies and subsequently to treatment arms within a sub-study, which gives explicit treatment allocation probabilities for every subject, sufficient for the identification of treatment effects and statistical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-321: The following is a narrative of Figure \ref{fig: schema (a) according to the order of enrollment windows.}
elements.sent-2411-323: All individuals are eligible to sub-study 1, while sub-study 2 is restricted to individuals with disease subtype A.
elements.sent-2411-324: Individuals in sub-study 1 are randomized to treatment 1 or 2.
elements.sent-2411-325: Individuals with subtype A are randomized to sub-study 1 or 2, and then randomized to treatment 1 or 3 if they are in sub-study 2.
elements.sent-2411-326: Following this addition, an individual with subtype A is randomly assigned to either sub-study 1, 2, or 3, and subsequently randomized to the treatments being assessed within the respective sub-study.
elements.sent-2411-327: Since treatment 3 has been demonstrated to be superior to treatment 1 for subtype A, it replaces treatment 1 as the control in sub-study 3.

elements.sent-2411-337: Consider a platform trial with $J$ treatments.
elements.sent-2411-338: For each individual, let $\bmW$ be a vector of all observed baseline variables (e.g., baseline covariates and enrollment window by calendar time) not affected by the treatment, $A$ be the treatment assignment indicator that equals $j$ if the individual is assigned to arm $j$, $Y^{(j)}$ be the potential outcome under treatment $j $, $j=1,...,J$ \citep{Neyman:1923a,Rubin:1974 , and $Y=Y^{(A)}$ be the observed outcome.}
elements.sent-2411-339: We assume throughout that $(\bmW_i, Y_i^{(1) , \dots, Y_i^{(J) , A_i)$, $i=1,\dots, n$, is an independent and identically distributed sample of size $n$ from $(\bmW, Y^{(1) , \dots, Y^{(J) , A)$ with finite second-order moments.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-340: The following is assumed for randomization in platform trials.
elements.sent-2411-341: However, in platform trials, this probability, $\pi_j(Z)$, varies depending on a baseline variable $Z$ and may be zero for some treatments.
elements.sent-2411-342: Typically, $Z$ is discrete and includes the enrollment window by calendar time and some key individual characteristics, as the enrollment window determines which treatments are available for enrollment, and individual characteristics determine the treatments for which an individual is eligible.
elements.sent-2411-343: Hence, $\pi_j(Z)$ may be zero if treatment $j$ has not entered the platform trial or has closed its enrollment, or if an individual does not meet the eligibility requirements for treatment $j$.
elements.sent-2411-344: Additionally, non-zero values of $\pi_j(Z)$ can differ over time and individual characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-345: The examples in Figure \ref{fig: schema illustrate these dynamics, where {$Z=(Z_{\rm win , Z_{\rm sub )$, $Z_{\rm win}$ represents the enrollment window by calendar time, and $Z_{\rm sub}$ is the disease subtype (A or not A).}}}}

elements.sent-2411-349: If randomized to sub-study 2, the individual is further randomized to treatment 1 or 3 with equal probability.

elements.sent-2411-353: Building on the discussion in Section 1.2, we now formally define the population of interest for comparing two treatments $j$ and $k$ (one of which is typically a control).
elements.sent-2411-354: In this paper, population specifically refers to the population of individuals used to define the estimand.
elements.sent-2411-355: Formally, the ECE population is a population of all individuals with $\pi_j(\bmZ) >0 $ and $ \pi_k(\bmZ)>0 $.
elements.sent-2411-356: It is important to note that the ECE population is a population of all individuals who could potentially be assigned to both treatments $j$ and $k$, rather than those who are actually assigned to these treatments.
elements.sent-2411-357: In traditional parallel-group trials, this distinction is often overlooked because the potential and actual populations coincide.
elements.sent-2411-358: However, in platform trials, this issue has been brought to the forefront.
elements.sent-2411-359: We now present two simple stylistic examples to illustrate why using the population consisting of who are actually assigned to the two treatments or sub-studies may lead to unexpected problems.
elements.sent-2411-360: In Example 1, where all participants are eligible for all treatments, the focus is on how changing treatment allocation probabilities over time can cause issues in defining the population.
elements.sent-2411-361: In Example 2, all treatments start enrollment simultaneously, and the focus is on how different eligibility criteria for different treatments complicates population definition.
elements.figure-2411-010: \spacingset{1.2} \includegraphics[width=.8\textwidth]{images/Picture1_v5.pdf} \caption{Comparison of traditional parallel-group and platform trials in a hypothetical trial population with mild to moderate disease in Example 1.} \label{fig: example table}
elements.sent-2411-362: Figure \ref{fig: example table outlines various trial designs for evaluating three treatments, with the entire trial population eligible for all treatments.}
elements.sent-2411-363: Over time, participant characteristics shift, with a higher proportion of participants having mild disease during enrollment window 2.
elements.sent-2411-364: We focus on the population for comparing treatment 2 versus treatment 1.
elements.sent-2411-365: If adopting a traditional parallel-group trial design (designs 1 and 1'), since every participant in the trial population has a positive probability of receiving either treatment, the ECE population is the entire trial population (comprising 50\% mild and 50\% moderate disease).
elements.sent-2411-366: This population is clearly the one to use when comparing treatments 1 and 2.
elements.sent-2411-367: In a platform trial design, the ECE population is still the same as the entire trial population, regardless of the platform trial type (design 2 vs. design 3) or specific randomization probabilities (design 2 vs. design 2').
elements.sent-2411-368: Therefore, the ECE population is reasonable, as the population for comparing treatments 1 and 2 should not change based on whether the trial follows a traditional parallel-group design or a platform trial of different types, nor should it be influenced by randomization probabilities, as long as the treatment availability and eligibility criteria remain the same.
elements.sent-2411-369: However, in platform trials, it is common to see statistical analyses that naively subset participants assigned to treatment 1 or 2 (in designs 2 and 2') or assigned to sub-study 1 (in design 3).
elements.sent-2411-370: These analyses target the population assigned to some specific treatments or sub-studies.
elements.sent-2411-371: While these are concurrently eligible populations, they are not the ECE population and can be unexpectedly influenced by the trial design and randomization ratios.
elements.sent-2411-372: For instance, in designs 2 and 2', changing the allocation probabilities during enrollment window 2 from (1/3, 1/3, 1/3) to (1/4, 1/4, 1/2) alters the analysis population from 47\% mild disease to 45\% mild disease.
elements.sent-2411-373: Similarly, switching from design 2 to design 3 changes the analysis population further, to 42\% mild disease.
elements.sent-2411-374: In contrast, the ECE population for treatments 1 and 2 remains the entire trial population, with 50\% mild disease.
elements.sent-2411-375: This illustrates that focusing only on the population assigned to specific arms or sub-studies can lead to unpredictable changes in population characteristics, which could be problematic.
elements.sent-2411-376: Lastly, we remark that the practice of focusing on populations assigned to specific treatments or sub-studies likely originates from traditional parallel-group trials.
elements.sent-2411-377: In traditional trials, these issues do not arise because the constant randomization ratio ensures that the population in any arm coincides with the ECE population.
elements.figure-2411-011: \spacingset{1.2} \includegraphics[width=.6\textwidth]{images/example2.pdf} \caption{A hypothetical platform trial in Example 2, with a population with different disease severity (mild, moderate, and severe).}
elements.sent-2411-378: Consider a platform trial in which all treatments begin enrollment simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-379: There are two sub-studies: one evaluates treatments 1 and 2 in a population with mild to moderate disease, and the other evaluates treatments 3 and 4 in a population with moderate to severe disease.
elements.sent-2411-380: The moderate disease population is randomized between the two sub-studies in a 1:1 ratio.
elements.sent-2411-381: These two populations are commonly of interest in the context of ulcerative colitis \citep{us2022ulcerative .}
elements.sent-2411-382: Suppose the overall disease population consists of 40\% mild, 40\% moderate, and 20\% severe disease.
elements.sent-2411-383: When studying treatments in the mild-to-moderate population, the trial should reflect the actual population composition—50\% mild and 50\% moderate disease—whether it is a traditional parallel-group trial or a platform trial.
elements.sent-2411-384: As shown in Figure 4, the ECE population for comparing treatments 1 and 2 exactly represents this population of interest, with 50\% mild and 50\% moderate disease.
elements.sent-2411-385: However, if we consider only those participants assigned to treatments 1 or 2, or those within sub-study 1, the population may arbitrarily depend on the randomization ratio between sub-studies.
elements.sent-2411-386: For instance, if the allocation probability of the moderate disease population to sub-study 1 changes from 1/2 to 1/4, the resulting population in sub-study 1 changes to 80\% mild and 20\% moderate disease.
elements.sent-2411-387: In summary, the ECE population is the largest population that preserves the integrity of randomized comparison and aligns with the intention-to-treat principle \citep{ICHE9 .}
elements.sent-2411-388: It is an appropriate population for both traditional and platform trials, and is especially useful for providing a clear and logical framework for defining populations when evaluating treatment effects in platform trials.
elements.sent-2411-389: In contrast, any population defined by actual treatment assignments or sub-study allocations may not be reasonable, as they could depend on trial design or specific randomization probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-390: Additionally, individuals outside the ECE population have zero probability of being assigned to either treatment $j$ or $k$, due to ineligibility or unavailability of treatments, thereby necessitating extrapolation.
elements.sent-2411-391: In some situations, other populations, such as subgroups defined by specific characteristics, enrollment windows, or re-weighted populations from different enrollment windows, may be of interest.
elements.sent-2411-392: For example, in COVID-19 trials with rapidly evolving variants, the population enrolled during later windows, when newer variants are prevalent, may hold the greatest clinical relevance.
elements.sent-2411-393: The ECE population weights each enrollment window according to its natural size, but alternative weights that reflect clinical priorities can also be considered.
elements.sent-2411-394: In any case, we recommend starting with the ECE population as the baseline, then considering its subgroups or applying weighting to reflect the target population of interest.
elements.sent-2411-395: This approach offers clarity and specificity in defining the population of interest and helps avoid potential pitfalls, as demonstrated in Examples 1 and 2.
elements.sent-2411-396: Moreover, the ECE population is crucial in other areas of statistical research, such as when using nonconcurrent controls to improve trial efficiency, as it provides a clear reference point for evaluating both efficiency gains and potential bias.
elements.sent-2411-397: These topics will be explored in future research.
elements.subsection-2411-018: Treatment eﬀect for the ECE population
elements.sent-2411-398: With the population now clearly defined, we propose the following estimand to define the treatment eﬀect for two given treatments $j$ and $k$ within the ECE population: \begin{equation}{\bm \vartheta}_{jk} = \left( \begin{array}{c} \theta_{jk} \theta_{kj} \end{array}\right) = \left( \begin{array}{c} E ( Y^{(j)} \mid \pi_{j}(\bmZ) >0, \pi_{k}(\bmZ)>0 ) E ( Y^{(k)} \mid \pi_{j}(\bmZ)>0, \pi_{k}(\bmZ)>0 ) \end{array}\right) , \label{effect1}\end{equation} where $\theta_{jk}$ and $\theta_{kj}$ depend on $j$ and $k$ because the ECE population depends on them.
elements.sent-2411-399: A contrast of the estimand in \eqref{effect1 is an unconditional treatment effect for the ECE population, averaging across all eligible participants and over time during which the enrollment window is open.}
elements.sent-2411-400: For example, the treatment effect of $j$ versus $k$ can be defined as the linear contrast $\theta_{jk - \theta_{kj}$, the ratio $\theta_{jk /\theta_{kj}$, or some other function of ${\bm \vartheta _{jk}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-401: Due to differences in the populations, $\theta_{jk}$ and $\theta_{kj}$ in \eqref{effect1 differ from $E(Y^{(j) )$ and $E(Y^{(k) )$, respectively, which are the means for the entire platform trial population and may include individuals who are non-concurrent or ineligible for treatments $j$ and $k$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-402: Additionally, the estimand in \eqref{effect1 differs from the treatment effect for those assigned to specific arms/sub-studies, as it truly respects the eligibility criteria for the given pair of treatments being compared, rather than on the subset assigned to particular arms/sub-studies.}
elements.sent-2411-403: The analysis set, distinct from the population used to define the estimand, refers to the sample used in statistical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-404: While the ECE population is uniquely specified, the analysis set is not unique and can include multiple options.
elements.sent-2411-405: To maximize efficiency, the analysis set can include all concurrently eligible individuals in the index set ${{{\cal I _{jk = \{ i : \pi_j(\bmZ_i) > 0, \pi_k(\bmZ_i) > 0 \}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-406: However, practical considerations may lead to using a subset of ${{{\cal I _{jk}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-407: For example, when applying a partial blinding strategy \citep{fda:2023platform or if other sub-studies remain blinded at the time of analysis, it may be preferable to restrict the analysis set to individuals within a specific sub-study.}
elements.sent-2411-408: In these cases, the analysis set is a biased sample of the ECE population and requires appropriate corrections.
elements.sent-2411-409: A general discussion of analysis sets and the conditions they must satisfy is provided in Section S2 of the Supplement.
elements.sent-2411-410: To focus our discussions, in the following, we consider either the analysis set defined by ${{{\cal I _{jk}$ (with covariate adjustment) or the subset ${{{\cal I _{jk \cap \{i: A_i = j \text{ or A_i = k\}$ (without covariate adjustment), where $\cap$ denotes the intersection of two sets.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-411: After we define the estimand, the next challenge is estimating each component of ${\bm \vartheta _{jk}$ in (\ref{effect1 ), because even among the concurrently eligible individuals defined by $\pi_{j (\bmZ)>0$ and $ \pi_{k (\bmZ)>0 $, these non-zero probabilities $\pi_{j (\bmZ)$ and $ \pi_{k (\bmZ)$ may still vary over time and individual characteristics, leading to confounding.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-412: A naive method is to estimate the component $\theta_{jk $ by the sample mean $\bar{Y _{jk}$ of $Y_i$'s from individuals with $\pi_j(\bmZ_i)>0$, $\pi_k(\bmZ_i)>0$, and $A_i=j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-413: However, $\bar{Y _{jk}$ is typically biased for $\theta_{jk}$ under platform trials as it overlooks confounding by $\bmZ$, i.e., the fact that $Z$ affects both treatment and outcome.}
elements.sent-2411-414: Specifically, as shown in the Supplement, as $n \to \infty$, \begin{equation}\bar{Y}_{jk} \to \frac{E\{ \pi_j(\bmZ) E( Y^{(j)} \mid \bmZ) \mid \pi_j(\bmZ)>0, \pi_k(\bmZ)>0 \}}{ E\{ \pi_j(\bmZ)\mid \pi_j(\bmZ)>0, \pi_k(\bmZ)>0\}} \quad in probability . \label{effect2}\end{equation} The limit of $\bar{Y _{jk}$ in (\ref{effect2 ) is not equal to $\theta_{jk}$ in (\ref{effect1 ) unless either $\pi_j(\bmZ)$ does not vary with $\bmZ$ or $Y^{(j) \perp \bmZ$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-415: Take the trial described in Figure \ref{fig: schema as an example, where $\pi_j(\bmZ)$ varies with $\bmZ$ (which does not occur in traditional clinical trials), the condition $Y^{(j) \perp \bmZ$ unrealistically rules out the influence of disease subtype on the outcome, as well as potential temporal effects, such as later recruited individuals being healthier than those recruited earlier.}}
elements.sent-2411-416: As noted in Section 4.2, the naive approach is biased because it does not account for confounding by $Z$.
elements.sent-2411-417: Under Assumption \ref{assump: randomization , since the true probability of receiving treatment, $\pi_j(Z)$, is known, this confounding can be addressed through inverse probability weighting (IPW).}

elements.sent-2411-482: The platform trial offers the potential to enhance statistical efficiency by facilitating the sharing of data between different treatment evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-483: However, realizing this potential requires careful consideration of two fundamental challenges: defining meaningful estimands to reflect eligibility and concurrency, and developing robust estimation methods to accommodate the treatment assignment probabilities varying with time and individual characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-484: In this article, we present a clear framework for constructing a clinically meaningful estimand with precise specification of the population.
elements.sent-2411-485: Our main discussion centers on comparing two treatments within the ECE population, as it is the largest population that preserves the integrity of randomization and avoids arbitrary dependence on randomization probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-486: This framework is adaptable to various contexts, such as comparisons across multiple treatments and evaluation of treatment effects within specific subgroups or a re-weighted population.

elements.sent-2411-565: Then, for any bivariate vectors $\bm t$ and $\bm s$, \begin{equation}\ P(\sqrt{n_{jk}}\bm U_{jk}\leq \bm t, \sqrt{n_{jk}}\bm V_{jk}\leq \bm s) = \ E\left[ E \left\{I(\sqrt{n_{jk}}\bm U_{jk}\leq \bm t, \sqrt{n_{jk}}\bm V_{jk}\leq \bm s)\mid I_{jk} (Z_i), i=1,\dots, n, {\cal S, A} \right\}\right] = \ E\left\{ I( \sqrt{n_{jk}}\bm V_{jk}\leq \bm s) E \{I(\sqrt{n_{jk}}\bm U_{jk}\leq \bm t ) \mid I_{jk} (Z_i), i=1,\dots, n, {\cal S, A} \}\right\} = \ E\left\{ I( \sqrt{n_{jk}}\bm V_{jk}\leq \bm s)\left[ \, P (\sqrt{n_{jk}}\bm U_{jk}\leq \bm t \mid I_{jk} (Z_i), i=1,\dots, n, {\cal S, A} ) - P(\bm\psi_U\leq \bm t) \right] \right\} \quad + P( \sqrt{n_{jk}}\bm V_{jk}\leq \bm s) P(\bm\psi_U\leq \bm t) \to \ P( \bm\psi_V \leq \bm s) P(\bm\psi_U\leq \bm t)\end{equation} where the second equality follows from the fact that $\bm V_{jk}$ is non-random conditional on $ \{I_{jk (Z_i), i=1,\dots, n\}$, ${\cal S, A}$, and the last step is from the bounded convergence theorem.}

elements.subsection-2411-022: Treatment effect for the ECE population
elements.sent-2411-647: With the population now clearly defined, we propose the following estimand to define the treatment effect for two given treatments $j$ and $k$ within the ECE population: \begin{equation}{\bm \vartheta}_{jk} = \left( \begin{array}{c} \theta_{jk} \theta_{kj} \end{array}\right) = \left( \begin{array}{c} E ( Y^{(j)} \mid \pi_{j}(\bmZ) >0, \pi_{k}(\bmZ)>0 ) E ( Y^{(k)} \mid \pi_{j}(\bmZ)>0, \pi_{k}(\bmZ)>0 ) \end{array}\right) , \label{effect1}\end{equation} where $\theta_{jk}$ and $\theta_{kj}$ depend on $j$ and $k$ because the ECE population depends on them.
elements.sent-2411-648: Our main discussion centers on comparing two treatments within the ECE population, as it preserves the integrity of randomization and avoids arbitrary dependence on randomization probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-649: Heagerty$^1$, Ting Ye$^1$\footnote[2]{ Correspond to tingye1@uw.edu.}
elements.sent-2411-650: While offering increased flexibility, this constrained and non-uniform treatment assignment poses inferential challenges, with two fundamental ones being the precise definition of treatment effects and robust, efficient inference on these effects.
elements.sent-2411-651: Such challenges arise primarily because some commonly used analysis approaches may target estimands defined on populations inadvertently depending on randomization ratios or trial operation format, thereby undermining interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-652: This article, for the first time, presents a formal framework for constructing a clinically meaningful estimand with precise specification of the population of interest.
elements.sent-2411-653: Specifically, the proposed entire concurrently eligible (ECE) population not only preserves the integrity of randomized comparisons but also remains invariant to both the randomization ratio and trial operation format.
elements.sent-2411-654: Then, we develop weighting and post-stratification methods to estimate treatment effects under the same minimal assumptions used in traditional randomized trials.
elements.sent-2411-655: We also consider model-assisted covariate adjustment to fully unlock the efficiency potential of platform trials while maintaining robustness against model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-656: For all proposed estimators, we derive asymptotic distributions and propose robust variance estimators and compare them in theory and through simulations.
elements.sent-2411-657: The SIMPLIFY trial, a master protocol assessing continuation versus discontinuation of two common therapies in cystic fibrosis, is utilized to further highlight the practical significance of this research.
elements.sent-2411-658: All analyses are conducted using the R package RobinCID.
elements.sent-2411-659: The traditional paradigm of conducting a separate clinical trial for every investigational treatment has become ever more expensive and challenging \citep{woodcock2017master .}
elements.sent-2411-660: Master protocol trials have emerged as innovative designs to accelerate the evaluation of potentially effective treatments \citep{woodcock2017master .}
elements.sent-2411-661: These trials evaluate one or more treatments across one or more diseases within an overarching study structure.
elements.sent-2411-662: Three major types of master protocol designs are umbrella trials, platform trials, and basket trials.
elements.sent-2411-663: Umbrella trials evaluate multiple therapies for a single disease \citep{rashdan2016going, renfro2017statistical , basket trials evaluate a medical product for multiple diseases or disease subtypes \citep{redig2015basket, hobbs2022basket , while platform trials offer the added flexibility of adding or removing treatment arms over time \citep{berry2015platform, saville2016efficiencies, park2020overview, gold2022platform, burki2023platform, pitre2023methodology .}}}
elements.sent-2411-664: These three types are not mutually exclusive, and a master protocol trial can incorporate features of more than one type.
elements.sent-2411-665: For example, an umbrella-platform hybrid for lung cancer might test different treatments across biomarker-defined subtypes (umbrella) while allowing treatments to enter or exit over time (platform).
elements.sent-2411-666: Similarly, a basket-platform hybrid might study a single targeted therapy across multiple cancers (basket), with new cancer types or biomarkers added adaptively over time (platform).
elements.sent-2411-667: Therefore, umbrella and basket trials can be viewed as special cases of platform trials, and, in this paper, we use the terms platform trial and master protocol interchangeably.
elements.sent-2411-668: Master protocols originated in oncology and remain widely used in this field, where many new biomarker-driven therapies require efficient evaluation \citep{park2019systematic,park2020overview,ouma2022design .}
elements.sent-2411-669: By utilizing a common screening platform to efficiently screen for biomarkers and identify all treatments for which a patient is eligible, these trial designs significantly improve operational efficiency, accelerate patient accrual, and enable the study of rare tumor subtypes within one trial \citep{woodcock2017master .}
elements.sent-2411-670: In addition to streamlining operations, these designs also have the potential to improve statistical efficiency by facilitating data sharing across different treatment evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-671: Notable examples include the BATTLE umbrella trials \citep{kim2011battle, papadimitrakopoulou2016battle , the Lung-MAP platform trial \citep{herbst2015lung , and the I-SPY 2 platform trial \citep{barker2009spy .}}}
elements.sent-2411-672: The utility of master protocol trials is much broader.
elements.sent-2411-673: They are gaining prominence in other diseases areas, including infectious diseases \citep{normand2021recovery , neurodegenerative diseases such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis \citep{quintana2023design and Alzheimer’s disease \citep{bateman2017dian , as well as inflammatory bowel disease \citep{honap2024basket .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-674: Section 2 presents two trials to provide concrete illustrations.
elements.sent-2411-675: Compared to traditional parallel-group trials, master protocol trials often share a key feature: constrained and non-uniform treatment assignment.
elements.sent-2411-676: Constrained means some participants have zero probability of receiving certain treatments, while non-uniform refers to varying non-zero assignment probabilities based on participant characteristics, enrollment time, or treatment availability.
elements.sent-2411-677: This feature marks a key distinction between master protocol and traditional trial designs, and poses significant challenges for the interpretability of and statistical inference for the treatment effect, some of which are highlighted in a recent draft guidance from the U.S.
elements.sent-2411-679: Although master protocols are increasingly conducted, two fundamental questions arising from their constrained and non-uniform treatment assignment remain unresolved.. 1.
elements.sent-2411-680: In master protocol trials, treatments often have different eligibility criteria and can be added or removed over time, leading to changes in the set of available treatment options throughout the trial.
elements.sent-2411-681: Consequently, the set of treatments to which an individual can be randomized and the corresponding assignment probabilities can vary over time and across individual characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-682: This leads to constrained and non-uniform treatment assignments.
elements.sent-2411-683: As a result, defining an estimand becomes more challenging because the treatment effect must be interpreted in the context of restricted eligibility, variable assignment probabilities, and evolving trial structure over time.
elements.sent-2411-684: In particular, among the five estimand attributes (\cite{ICHE9-R1 ), the target population of interest requires special attention.}
elements.sent-2411-685: However, this general recommendation does not uniquely define the population, leading to ambiguity.
elements.sent-2411-686: For instance, in Cases I-II in Section 3.2, a seemingly reasonable population (and thus the estimand) may depend arbitrarily on randomization probabilities, even though it technically follows the general recommendation in \cite{fda:2023platform .}
elements.sent-2411-687: To address this question, Section 3.2 introduces a population for comparing two treatments (typically with one as a control) that not only follows the general recommendations in \cite{fda:2023platform but also respects the eligibility criteria while avoiding arbitrary dependencies on randomization probabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-688: This definition also applies to traditional parallel-group trials, to which we draw simple connections using Case I in Section 3.2 to explain why this population is sensible.
elements.sent-2411-689: As in traditional trials, the target population in a master protocol trial may sometimes be a specific subgroup or a re-weighted population.
elements.sent-2411-690: In such cases, the ECE population can serve as the reference and may be restricted to the relevant subgroup or re-weighted to resemble the target population.
elements.sent-2411-691: Our proposal is the first in the literature to offer a systematic framework for defining clinically meaningful estimands with clear specification of the population of interest.
elements.sent-2411-692: Given the central importance of the estimand, our contribution enhances the fundamental understanding of master protocol trials and lays the groundwork for future statistical research—both frequentist and Bayesian—on topics such as interim analysis and the use of nonconcurrent controls.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-693: How to provide robust estimation and inference regarding the defined treatment effect? Although randomization is preserved, the commonly used estimator in traditional trials—comparing outcome means between treatment arms—can easily lead to biased estimates in master protocol trials, where treatment assignment is often constrained and non-uniform, potentially giving rise to issues such as Simpson’s paradox \citep{collignon2020current, dodd2021platform, lee2021statistical, meyer2021systematic .}
elements.sent-2411-694: Consequently, estimating treatment effects in master protocol trials is not straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-695: A promising first step is to adapt inverse probability weighting or post-stratification to account for the constrained and non-uniform treatment assignment.
elements.sent-2411-696: Additionally, model-assisted covariate adjustment can offer another means to further unlock the efficiency potential of master protocol trials, while maintaining robustness against model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-697: To guide practitioners in selecting among these methods, we derive the asymptotic variance and relative efficiency of these estimation methods.
elements.sent-2411-698: These results are novel and, to our knowledge, have not previously appeared in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-699: The increasing popularity of platform trials has led to a surge of research addressing their statistical challenges in both design and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-700: A key concern in the analysis of platform trials is the potential presence of time trends, which can introduce bias when using nonconcurrent controls \citep{bofill2023use .}
elements.sent-2411-701: However, all of these statistical methods have predominantly relied on correct modeling and are sensitive to model misspecification.
elements.sent-2411-702: The platform trial literature on non-model-based approaches for concurrently eligible individuals remains limited.
elements.sent-2411-703: More recently, \cite{santacatterina2025identification defined an estimand based on the concurrent period and established identification and estimation results using only concurrent controls.}
elements.sent-2411-704: They also proposed strategies to incorporate nonconcurrent controls under a strong conditional exchangeability assumption between concurrent and nonconcurrent controls, given observed covariates.
elements.sent-2411-705: However, none of these studies consider the more general setting in which treatments have distinct eligibility criteria, nor do they rigorously investigate how to define a clinically meaningful estimand within a general framework that is independent of specific study designs or modeling assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-706: Section 2 presents the SIMPLIFY trial and a stylized trial used to emphasize key issues.
elements.sent-2411-707: Section 3 details the ECE population and corresponding estimand.
elements.sent-2411-708: In Section \ref{sec: theory , we establish the asymptotic distributions and relative efficiency of the proposed estimators.}
elements.sent-2411-709: We highlight the practical significance through the SIMPLIFY trial in Section \ref{sec: real data , and we conclude with a summary and recommendations in Section \ref{sec: discussion .}}
elements.sent-2411-710: We begin by presenting the real master protocol trial that motivates this work: the SIMPLIFY trial \citep{mayer2023discontinuation .}
elements.sent-2411-711: Although it resembles an umbrella trial, where multiple treatments are evaluated concurrently and have different eligibility criteria, it also presents a key feature of platform trials: varying randomization ratio over time.
elements.sent-2411-712: Despite its seemingly simple design diagram, the trial involves both constrained and non-uniform treatment assignments.
elements.sent-2411-713: Its clarity makes it ideal for highlighting the two fundamental gaps, as it facilitates understanding of the underlying challenges.
elements.sent-2411-714: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is caused by mutations in the CF transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) gene.
elements.sent-2411-715: CFTR modulator therapies are a class of small-molecule drugs designed to address the underlying cause of CF, leading to transformative improvements in patient outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-716: A landmark example is the triple-combination therapy elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), approved for individuals with at least one F508del allele, a mutation present in over 85\% of people with CF.
elements.sent-2411-717: Given the potential overlap in therapeutic effects between ETI and pre-existing treatments such as hypertonic saline (HS) and dornase alfa (DA) and the need to reduce treatment burden, it is important to evaluate the impact of discontinuing these pre-existing therapies in individuals taking ETI.
elements.sent-2411-718: The SIMPLIFY trial was conducted within this context.
elements.sent-2411-719: It includes two concurrently run multi-center randomized controlled studies at 80 participating clinics across the United States in the CF Therapeutics Development Network.
elements.sent-2411-720: The primary objective for each study is to determine whether discontinuing either HS or DA, respectively, is non-inferior to continuing therapy in individuals using the highly eﬀective modulator ETI.
elements.figure-2411-016: \spacingset{1.2} \includegraphics[scale=.65]{images/SIMPLIFY.pdf} \caption{\label{fig: simplify} SIMPLIFY design schema. Individuals on a single therapy enter into the corresponding study with probability 1, while those on both are randomized to the HS or DA study, with ratio of 1:1 during enrollment window (EW) 1, then 3:1 during EW 2. Within HS (or DA) study, individuals are randomized 1:1 to continue or discontinue HS (or DA).}

elements.sent-2411-721: All enrolled individuals have initiated ETI for at least 90 days and belong to one of three groups based on medication use when entering the trial: those on HS only, DA only, or both therapies.
elements.sent-2411-722: Individuals on a single therapy (HS only or DA only) are assigned to the corresponding study with probability 1.
elements.sent-2411-723: Those on both therapies are randomized to the HS or DA study, with 1:1 ratio during initial enrollment window 1 (before January 14, 2022), and 3:1 ratio during enrollment window 2 (after January 14, 2022) to assign more participants to the HS study, as the DA study was enrolling more quickly.
elements.sent-2411-724: This swift shift in the randomization ratio exemplifies how the flexibility of master protocol trials can accelerate clinical trials.
elements.sent-2411-725: Within each study (HS or DA), participants were then randomized 1:1 to either continue or discontinue their respective therapy.
elements.sent-2411-726: Table \ref{table: prob simplify summarizes assignment probabilities of the SIMPLIFY trial.}
elements.sent-2411-727: It illustrates both constrained and non-uniform treatment assignment: participants receiving only HS or DA have zero probability of being assigned to certain treatment arms, and the non-zero probabilities of receiving any given pair of treatments may vary based on baseline characteristics and enrollment windows.
