elements.sent-2411-729: To motivate a general statistical framework for master protocol trials, we introduce a stylistic trial in two operation formats, both can be unified under our proposed framework.
elements.sent-2411-730: This unification highlights an important point: while trial design may influence clinical operations, it should not alter the clinical questions being addressed.

elements.sent-2411-747: The ECE population is a population of all individuals who could potentially be assigned to treatments $j$ and $k$, rather than the population represented by those actually assigned to these treatments (i.e., the population represented by $A\in \{j, k\}$).
elements.sent-2411-748: In traditional trials, this distinction is often overlooked because the potential and actual populations coincide.
elements.sent-2411-749: We present two basic cases to illustrate why using the population represented by those actually assigned to the two treatments or sub-studies may lead to unexpected problems.
elements.sent-2411-750: In Case I, all participants are eligible for all treatments, and the focus is on how changing treatment allocation probabilities over time (non-uniform treatment assignment) can cause issues in defining the population.
elements.sent-2411-751: In Case II, all treatments enroll concurrently, and the focus is on how different eligibility criteria for different treatments (constrained treatment assignment) complicates population definition.
elements.sent-2411-752: Finally, we revisit the SIMPLIFY trial, which features both constrained and non-uniform treatment assignment, reflected in time-varying treatment allocation probabilities and varying eligibility criteria across treatments.
elements.figure-2411-019: \spacingset{1.2} \includegraphics[width=.85\textwidth]{images/CaseI.png} \caption{Comparison of traditional parallel-group and platform trial designs in a hypothetical mild to moderate disease population (Case I). In this case, the ECE population for comparing treatments 2 and 1 coincides with the entire trial population across all designs. In contrast, in platform trials, the population represented by those assigned to specific treatments or sub-studies may vary arbitrarily depending on the trial design or randomization probabilities.} \label{fig: example table}
elements.sent-2411-753: The entire trial population is 50\% mild and 50\% moderate, but patient characteristics shift over time: enrollment window 1 representing one third of the population consists of 30\% mild and 70\% moderate, while enrollment window 2 comprising the remaining two third includes 60\% mild and 40\% moderate.
elements.sent-2411-754: We focus on the population for comparing treatments 2 and 1, as these two treatments are open for enrollment during the same period across all designs.
elements.sent-2411-755: If a traditional parallel-group trial design (Design 1) is used, all participants have a positive probability of receiving either treatment, making the ECE population identical to the entire trial population (50\% mild and 50\% moderate).
elements.sent-2411-756: This is typically the default population for comparing treatments 1 and 2.
elements.sent-2411-757: The same applies to all platform trial designs in Figure \ref{fig: example table , where the ECE population remains identical to the entire trial population regardless of the specific platform trial type (Design 2 vs. 3) or randomization probabilities (Design 2 vs. 2’).}
elements.sent-2411-758: This consistency provides a principled justification for using the ECE population, as the target population for treatment comparisons should not depend on the specific trial operation formate or randomization probabilities, in the same way that choices of randomization ratio do not affect the study population in traditional trials.
elements.sent-2411-759: However, in platform trials, it is common to conduct analyses that naively subset participants assigned to treatment 1 or 2 (Design 2 and 2'), or to sub-study 1 (Design 3).
elements.sent-2411-760: Even without explicit subsetting, methods such as the t-test are often applied in Designs 2 and 2', where the randomization ratio between treatments 1 and 2 is constant.
elements.sent-2411-761: These analyses target the population of individuals assigned to specific treatments or sub-studies.
elements.sent-2411-762: Although such analysis populations still consist of concurrently eligible individuals, as shown in Figure \ref{fig: example table , they differ from the ECE population and can be unexpectedly influenced by design formats and randomization ratios.}

elements.figure-2411-020: \spacingset{1.2} \includegraphics[width=.75\textwidth]{images/example2.pdf} \caption{A hypothetical umbrella trial (Case II) with a population with different disease severity (mild, moderate, and severe). In this case, the ECE population for comparing treatments 2 and 1 is the mild-to-moderate disease population (with 50\% mild and 50\% moderate). In contrast, the analysis population defined by participants assigned to sub-study 1 may vary arbitrarily depending on how individuals with moderate disease are randomized between the two sub-studies.}
elements.sent-2411-764: The moderate disease population is randomized between the two sub-studies with a 1:1 ratio.
elements.sent-2411-765: When studying treatments for mild-to-moderate disease, the target population is 50\% mild and 50\% moderate.
elements.sent-2411-766: As shown in Figure 4, the ECE population for comparing treatments 1 and 2 includes all individuals with mild or moderate disease, since all can potentially be assigned to these two treatments, which exactly represents the population of interest -- 50\% mild and 50\% moderate.
elements.sent-2411-767: In contrast, if only considering those participants assigned to treatments 1 or 2, or those within sub-study 1, the analysis population may arbitrarily depend on the randomization ratio between sub-studies.
elements.sent-2411-768: For instance, if the allocation ratio to sub-study 1 for the moderate disease population decreases from 1/2 to 1/4, the resulting population restricted to sub-study 1 shifts to 80\% mild and 20\% moderate.
elements.sent-2411-769: The SIMPLIFY trial has two sub-studies: one compares treatments 1 and 2 in a population on HS only or both HS and DA; the other compares treatments 1 and 3 in a population on DA only or both HS and DA.
elements.sent-2411-770: Participants on both HS and DA are first randomized between the two sub-studies using a 1:1 ratio, which was later adjusted to 3:1.
elements.sent-2411-771: As shown in Figure \ref{fig: simplify , the ECE population for comparing treatments 1 and 2 (i.e., assessing the effect of discontinuing HS versus continuing) includes all individuals on HS only or on both HS and DA, irrespective of the sub-study randomization ratio.}
elements.sent-2411-772: This appropriately reflects the eligibility criteria for the treatments being compared.
elements.sent-2411-773: In contrast, a naive analysis restricted to participants assigned to the HS study targets an analysis population that differentially weights the HS-only and HS+DA groups, where the weights are determined by the randomization ratio among individuals on both HS and DA, as well as the timing of any changes to that ratio; further discussions are in section 6.
elements.sent-2411-774: Alternatively, an analysis that includes all participants assigned to treatments 1 or 2 may include DA-only individuals, making the resulting analysis population less relevant for evaluating the effect of discontinuing HS.
elements.sent-2411-775: In summary, the ECE population preserves the integrity of randomized comparison, is invariant to the randomization ratio or trial type, and aligns with the intention-to-treat principle \citep{ICHE9 .}
elements.sent-2411-776: The concept of ECE population can also be extended to comparisons involving more than two arms.
elements.sent-2411-777: For instance, the ECE population for comparing three treatments \( j \), \( k \), and \( l \) is a population of all individuals for whom \( \pi_j(Z) > 0 \), \( \pi_k(Z) > 0 \), and \( \pi_l(Z) > 0 \).
elements.sent-2411-778: This approach offers clarity and specificity in defining the population of interest, ensuring that the population reflects the clinical question rather than having arbitrary dependence on design choices like randomization ratio or trial types.
elements.sent-2411-779: In the context of the SIMPLIFY trial, $\theta_{21 -\theta_{12}$ is the effect of discontinuing HS versus continuing in a population of all individuals taking only HS or both HS and DA.}
elements.sent-2411-781: This estimand framework is readily extendable to settings with multiple treatment arms.
elements.sent-2411-782: Due to differences in the populations, $\theta_{jk}$ and $\theta_{kj}$ in \eqref{effect1 differ from $E(Y^{(j) )$ and $E(Y^{(k) )$, respectively, which are the means for the entire master protocol trial population and may include individuals who are non-concurrent or ineligible for treatments $j$ and $k$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-784: When these quantities are clinically of interest, one can apply methods to generalize the estimand in \eqref{effect1 to the entire trial population under additional assumptions \citep{degtiar2023review .}}
elements.sent-2411-785: Additionally, the estimand in \eqref{effect1 differs from the treatment effect for those assigned to specific arms (e.g., $E\{Y^{(j) -Y^{(k) \mid A \in(j, k)\}$), which, as shown by the formula, can depend on the distribution of $A$ and have problems such as arbitrary dependence on the randomization ratio.}}}
elements.sent-2411-786: Similarly, the estimand in \eqref{effect1 also differs from the treatment effect for those assigned to specific sub-studies.}
elements.sent-2411-787: A general discussion of analysis sets and the conditions they must satisfy is provided in Section S1 of the Supplement.
elements.sent-2411-788: This IPW estimator may be improved by the following stabilized IPW (SIPW) \citep{hajek1971comment estimator, which normalizes the weights $I(A_i=j) / \pi_j(\bmZ_i)$ for $ i \in {{{\cal I _{jk}$ to sum to 1: \begin{equation}\sipw = \bigg\{ \sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \frac{ I(A_i=j) }{\pi_j(\bmZ_i)}\bigg\}^{-1} \sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \frac{ I(A_i=j) Y_i }{\pi_j(\bmZ_i)} .\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-789: This robustness makes model-assisted approaches increasingly popular and recommended by the regulatory agencies \citep{fda:2019aa .}
elements.sent-2411-790: For example, in Figure \ref{fig: schema (a), when estimating the effect of treatment 3 versus treatment 1 at the end of enrollment window 2, the AIPW also incorporates covariate data from subtype A individuals assigned to treatment 2 (in sub-study 1) and treatment 4 (in sub-study 3) during enrollment windows 1 and 2.}
elements.sent-2411-791: These covariate data are not used in the IPW or SIPW estimators.
elements.sent-2411-792: Note that when $Z_i$ is discrete, $\pi_j(Z_i)$ can be estimated using sample proportions and used in place of the true value $\pi_j(Z)$ in any of the above weighting estimators.
elements.sent-2411-793: This approach is known as post-stratification in sample surveys \citep{fuller2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-794: It is important to note that post-stratification with redundant strata may lead to excessively small strata, adversely affecting the finite sample performance of PS estimator or its variance estimator; this is further explored through simulation results and discussed in Section S2 of the Supplement.
elements.sent-2411-795: This gives the adjusted post-stratification (APS) estimator of $\theta_{jk}$, \begin{equation}\hat\theta_{jk}^{\rm \, aps} = \frac{1}{n_{jk}} \sum_{h=1}^{H_{jk}} \frac{n({{\cal I}}_{jk}^{(h)})}{n_{j} ({{\cal I}}_{jk}^{(h)}) } \sum_{i\in {{\cal I}}_{jk}^{(h)} } I(A_i =j ) \{ Y_i - \hat\mu_{jk}(\bmX_i)\} + \frac{1}{n_{jk}} \sum_{i\in {{\cal I}}_{jk} } \hat\mu_{jk}(\bmX_i) .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-797: Similarly, $E_{jk (\cdot \mid S) =E_{kj (\cdot \mid S)$, $\var_{jk (\cdot \mid S)=\var_{kj (\cdot \mid S)$, and $\cov_{jk (\cdot \mid S)=\cov_{kj (\cdot \mid S)$ denote these quantities further conditioned on $S$, where $S$ is the covariate for post-stratification, i.e., for individual $i$, $S_i = {\cal I _{jk ^{(h)}$ if and only if $i \in {\cal I _{jk ^{(h)}$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-798: We also show that the AIPW and SAIPW estimators with \( \mu_{jk = E_{jk (Y^{(j) \mid \bm{X ) \) and \( \mu_{kj = E_{jk (Y^{(k) \mid \bm{X ) \) are asymptotically equivalent and both attain semiparametric efficiency.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-802: Nonetheless, SIPW is often more efficient in simulations (see Section S2 of the Supplement).

elements.sent-2411-804: Condition (\ref{U ) holds without loss of generality since it can always be achieved by replacing $\hat\mu_{jk (\bmX)$ with \begin{equation}\tilde \mu_{jk} (\bmX) = \hat \mu_{jk}(\bmX) + \frac{1}{n_{jk}(S) } \sum_{i\in {\cal I}_{jk}, A_i=j, S_i = S} \{ Y_i^{(j)} - \hat\mu_{jk}(\bmX_i) \} , \label{adju}\end{equation} where $n_{jk (S) $ is the number of elements in $\{ i \in {\cal I _{jk , A_i=j, S_i = S \}$.}}}}}

elements.figure-2411-021: \spacingset{1.2} \includegraphics[width=.65\textwidth]{images/efficiency.pdf} \caption{Efficiency comparisons among all robust estimators, with the corresponding corollary annotated for each comparison. } \label{fig:eff}
elements.sent-2411-807: As reviewed in Section \ref{subsec: simplify and shown in Figure \ref{fig: simplify , the SIMPLIFY trial \citep{mayer2023discontinuation consists of two sub-studies (the HS and DA studies) and a total of three treatments: continuing therapy (treatment 1, used as the control) in both the HS and DA studies, discontinuing HS (treatment 2) in the HS study, and discontinuing DA (treatment 3) in the DA study.}}}
elements.sent-2411-808: In our analysis, the ECE population for evaluating the effect of discontinuing HS versus continuing is the population of all individuals taking only HS or both HS and DA.
elements.sent-2411-809: Accordingly, the treatment effect of interest, $\theta_{21 - \theta_{12}$, is defined on this ECE population.}
elements.sent-2411-810: Demographic and baseline characteristics for participants in the two ECE populations are presented in Tables S1-S2 of the Supplement.
elements.sent-2411-811: Note that our estimand differ slightly from those in \cite{mayer2023discontinuation .}
elements.sent-2411-812: For the defined estimands, we consider the IPW, SIPW, SAIPW, PS, and APS estimators, as described in Section \ref{sec: estimation , {all of which can account for the different treatment assignment probabilities across $Z$} and are evaluated in the simulation study in Section S2 of the Supplement.}
elements.sent-2411-813: Here, $Z$ includes baseline use of HS, DA, and enrollment windows.
elements.sent-2411-814: For SAIPW and APS, $\hat\mu_{jk}$ is estimated by fitting a linear regression of $Y$ on covariates using data from treatment arm $j$ in $\mathcal{I _{jk}$.}
elements.sent-2411-815: The covariates include $Z$ and several other baseline variables: two continuous covariates, age and baseline ppFEV$_1$; three baseline binary covariates, sex, race (white or non-white), and ethnicity (Hispanic or Latino, or not Hispanic or Latino); and one three-category covariate: genotype (delta F508 homozygous, heterozygous, or other/unknown).
elements.sent-2411-816: As shown in Corollary 2 and simulations, when adjusting for covariates using linear working models, the AIPW estimator is numerically nearly identical to the SAIPW estimator and thus omitted.
elements.sent-2411-817: We also include the naive ANOVA as described in Section \ref{subsec: naive method , which is subject to confounding bias.}
elements.sent-2411-818: Results are shown in Figure \ref{fig: simplify result .}
elements.figure-2411-022: \spacingset{1.2} \includegraphics[width=.9\textwidth]{images/SIMPLIFY_result_fig_4.10.png} \caption{Estimates and 95\% confidence intervals (in \%) in the SIMPLIFY trial. Naive refers to the naive ANOVA method described in Section 4.2. ANOVA* and ANCOVA* are estimates based on data from each individual sub-study. IPW, SIPW, SAIPW, PS, and APS are the correct estimators for the estimand defined on the ECE population.} \label{fig: simplify result}
elements.sent-2411-819: For all five proposed estimators (IPW, SIPW, SAIPW, PS, and APS), all point estimates are similar, as they target the same estimand defined on the ECE population.
elements.sent-2411-820: Since all confidence intervals are on the right side of the pre-defined non-inferiority margin $-$3\% (the vertical dashed line in Figure \ref{fig: simplify result ), non-inferiority of discontinuing therapy can be clearly claimed for both HS and DA -- consistent with the findings reported in \cite{mayer2023discontinuation .}}
elements.sent-2411-821: IPW and SIPW produce similar confidence intervals, likely because the true effect is near zero in this non-inferiority study.
elements.sent-2411-822: SIPW and SAIPW, as well as PS and APS, also yield similar confidence intervals, as most covariates are only weakly associated with the outcome (\( R^2 \) between 0.023 and 0.1 across three arms).
elements.sent-2411-823: This likely also explains why the naive estimator does not exhibit substantial bias in this analysis.
elements.sent-2411-824: For comparison, we also include the ANOVA* (sample means) and ANCOVA* estimators (using linear working models to adjust for the same covariates included in SAIPW and APS separately for each arm) based solely on data from each individual sub-study.
elements.sent-2411-825: As discussed in Section \ref{subsec: population , these estimators target analysis populations assigned to the HS or DA study.}
elements.sent-2411-826: As a result, the interpretations of these estimators differ from the five proposed estimators.
elements.sent-2411-827: Specifically, for the HS comparison, the HS study includes 18 HS-only and 222 HS+DA participants, while the sample from the ECE population includes 18 HS-only and 427 HS+DA participants.
elements.sent-2411-828: For the DA comparison, the DA study includes 139 DA-only and 205 HS+DA participants, whereas the sample from the ECE population includes 139 DA-only and 427 HS+DA participants.
elements.sent-2411-829: More detailed demographic and baseline characteristics for participants in the HS and DA studies are presented in Tables S3 and S4.
elements.sent-2411-830: The larger population difference in the DA comparison likely explains the more noticeable discrepancy in point estimates for that result (opposite sign relative to the five proposed estimators).
elements.sent-2411-831: Moreover, as expected, the ANOVA* and ANCOVA* estimators yield wider confidence intervals compared to the five proposed estimators.
elements.sent-2411-832: This is because, for the five proposed estimators, participants on both HS and DA assigned to treatment 1 (\(n = 214\)) contribute to both the treatment 2 vs. 1 and treatment 3 vs. 1 comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-833: The covariate-adjusted methods (SAIPW and APS) further leverage covariate information from all participants on both HS and DA (\(n = 427\)) in both comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-834: In contrast, ANOVA* and ANCOVA* split these participants between the HS and DA sub-studies, reducing the sample size for each comparison.
elements.sent-2411-835: As a result, the five proposed estimators achieve approximately 5\%–27\% variance reduction relative to the sub-study-specific estimators.
elements.sent-2411-836: Overall, the results from the SIMPLIFY trial highlight the critical importance of clearly defining the estimand in umbrella and platform trials.
elements.sent-2411-837: Without this clarity, estimators may have interpretations that depend on the randomization ratio or trial design.
elements.sent-2411-838: An additional advantage of the five proposed estimators is the potential for efficiency gains through robust use of pooled data across sub-studies.
elements.sent-2411-839: Master protocol trials have the potential to enhance statistical efficiency by facilitating the sharing of data across treatment evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-840: Lastly, our results are applicable not only to master protocol trials but also more broadly to trials with constrained or non-uniform treatment assignment.. \spacingset{.7 \bibliographystyle{apalike.}}

elements.subsection-2411-029: More discussions of Figure \ref{fig: example table}
elements.sent-2411-848: We present two additional scenarios based on Figure \ref{fig: example table :.}
elements.sent-2411-849: In Figure 3, Design 2, if we compare TRT 1 vs. TRT 3, the ECE population corresponds to the population in Enrollment Window 2 (comprising 60\% mild and 40\% moderate disease), which is different from the overall trial population.
elements.sent-2411-850: This is expected, as TRT 3 is not available during Enrollment Window 1.
elements.sent-2411-851: In Figure 3, Design 2, suppose TRT 4 is introduced in Enrollment Window 1 but discontinued in Enrollment Window 2 due to futility.
elements.sent-2411-852: In this case, the ECE population for comparing TRT 3 vs. TRT 4 would be empty, as no patients are concurrently eligible for both treatments within the same enrollment window.
elements.sent-2411-853: Thus, a direct randomized comparison is not possible.
elements.sent-2411-855: Analogous to a traditional trial setting, one would conduct two separate studies for TRT 3 and TRT 4 at different times in distinct populations.
elements.sent-2411-856: Any comparison between the two would then require indirect methods, which rely on strong assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-857: This scenario has implications for both the design and analysis of platform trials.
elements.sent-2411-858: At the design stage, if a particular comparison is of interest, the ECE framework can be used to ensure the existence of a non-empty ECE population, thereby enabling at least a subpopulation in which randomized comparison is feasible.
elements.sent-2411-859: At the analysis stage, one must proceed with extra caution and rely on additional assumptions — for example, by applying methods that incorporate non-concurrent controls or external controls \citep{pocock1976combination .}
elements.sent-2411-860: While this lies outside the scope of the current paper, which focuses on fully robust randomized comparisons, it represents an important area for future research.
elements.sent-2411-861: The consistency of the variance estimators is established in Section S3.
elements.sent-2411-862: For IPW-type estimators, we propose the following estimators of covariance matrices: \begin{equation}\hat \bS_{jk}^{\rm \, ipw} \! = \! \frac{1}{n_{jk}} \sum_{i \in {\cal I}_{jk}} \! {\rm diag} \! \left\{ {\textstyle \frac{I(A_i=j)Y_i^2}{\pi_j^2(\bmZ_i) }, \, \frac{I(A_i=k)Y_i^2}{\pi_k^2(\bmZ_i)} } \right\} - \hat{\vartheta}_{jk} \hat{\vartheta}_{jk}^T , \hat \bS_{jk}^{\rm \, sipw} \! = \! \frac{1}{n_{jk}}\sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \! {\rm diag} \! \left\{ {\textstyle \frac{I(A_i=j)(Y_i-\hat\theta_{jk})^2}{\pi_j^2(\bmZ_i)}, \, \frac{I(A_i=k)(Y_i-\hat\theta_{kj})^2}{\pi_k^2(\bmZ_i)} } \right\} , \hat \bS_{jk}^{\rm \, aipw} \! = \! \frac{1}{n_{jk}}\sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \! {\rm diag} \! \left\{ {\textstyle \frac{I(A_i=j)\{ Y_i-\hat\mu_{jk}(\bmX_i)\}^2}{\pi_j^2(\bmZ_i)} , \, \frac{I(A_i=k)\{ Y_i-\hat\mu_{kj}(\bmX_i) \}^2 }{\pi_k^2(\bmZ_i)} } \right\} + \hat{\bm\Lambda}_{jk} - \hat{\bm\delta}_{jk} \hat{\bm\delta}_{jk}^T , \hat \bS_{jk}^{\rm \, saipw} \! = \! \frac{1}{n_{jk}}\sum_{i \in {{{\cal I}}}_{jk}} \! {\rm diag} \! \left\{ {\textstyle \frac{I(A_i=j)\{ Y_i- \hat\mu_{jk}(\bmX_i) - \hat\delta_{jk} \}^2}{\pi_j^2(\bmZ_i)} , \, \frac{I(A_i=k)\{ Y_i- \hat\mu_{kj}(\bmX_i) -\hat\delta_{kj} \}^2}{\pi_k^2(\bmZ_i)} } \right\} + \hat{\bm\Lambda}_{jk} ,\end{equation} where $\hat{\bm{\vartheta _{jk = (\hat\theta_{jk , \hat\theta_{kj )^T$, $\hat\theta_{jk}$ is any estimator of $\theta_{jk}$, $ \hat{\bm\delta _{jk = (\hat\delta_{jk , \hat\delta_{kj )^T$, $\hat\delta_{jk = n_{jk ^{-1}\sum_{i \in {{{\cal I _{jk }\! I(A_i=j)\{Y_i - \hat\mu_{jk (\bmX_i)\}/\pi_j(\bmZ_i) $, $\hat{\bm\Lambda _{jk}$ is $\bm\Lambda_{jk}$ with $\lambda_{jk}$ and $c_{jk}$ respectively estimated by $\hat\lambda_{jk = 2 \hat q_{jk ^{(j) - \hat{\sigma ^2_{jk}$ and $\hat{c _{jk = \hat q_{kj ^{(j) + \hat q_{jk ^{(k) - \hat{q _{jk}$, \begin{equation}\hat q_{jk}^{(j)} = \frac{1}{n_{jk}}\sum_{i\in {\cal I}_{jk}}\frac{I(A_i=j)Y_i\hat\mu_{jk}(\bmX_i)}{\pi_j(Z_i)}-\frac{1}{n_{jk}^2}\sum_{i\in{\cal I}_{jk}}\frac{I(A_i=j)Y_i}{\pi_j(Z_i)}\sum_{i\in{\cal I}_{jk}}\frac{I(A_i=j)\hat\mu_{jk}(\bmX_i)}{\pi_j(Z_i)},\end{equation} $\hat q_{kj ^{(j)}$ is $\hat q_{jk ^{(j)}$ with $\hat\mu_{jk (\bmX_i)$ switched to $\hat\mu_{kj (\bmX_i)$, $\hat q_{jk ^{(k) $ is $\hat q_{jk ^{(j)}$ with $I(A_i = j) $ and $\pi_j(Z_i)$ replaced by $I(A_i = k) $ and $\pi_k(Z_i)$, respectively, \begin{equation}\hat{\sigma}^2_{jk}=\frac{1}{n_{jk}}\sum_{i\in {\cal I}_{jk}}\frac{I(A_i=j)\hat\mu^2_{jk}(\bmX_i)}{\pi_j(Z_i)}-\bigg\{\frac{1}{n_{jk}}\sum_{i\in{\cal I}_{jk}}\frac{I(A_i=j)\hat\mu_{jk}(\bmX_i)}{\pi_j(Z_i)}\bigg\}^2,\end{equation} $\hat{\sigma ^2_{kj}$ is $\hat{\sigma ^2_{jk}$ with $j$ and $k$ swapped, and \begin{equation}\hat q_{jk} =\frac{1}{2}\bigg\{ \frac{1}{n_{jk}}\sum_{i\in {\cal I}_{jk}}\frac{I(A_i=j)\hat\mu_{jk}(\bmX_i)\hat\mu_{kj}(\bmX_i)}{\pi_j(Z_i)}-\frac{1}{n_{jk}^2}\sum_{i\in{\cal I}_{jk}}\frac{I(A_i=j)\hat\mu_{jk}(\bmX_i)}{\pi_j(Z_i)}\sum_{i\in{\cal I}_{jk}}\frac{I(A_i=j)\hat\mu_{kj}(\bmX_i)}{\pi_j(Z_i)}\bigg\} +\frac{1}{2}\bigg\{ \frac{1}{n_{jk}}\sum_{i\in {\cal I}_{jk}}\frac{I(A_i=k)\hat\mu_{jk}(\bmX_i)\hat\mu_{kj}(\bmX_i)}{\pi_k(Z_i)}-\frac{1}{n_{jk}^2}\sum_{i\in{\cal I}_{jk}}\frac{I(A_i=k)\hat\mu_{jk}(\bmX_i)}{\pi_k(Z_i)}\sum_{i\in{\cal I}_{jk}}\frac{I(A_i=k)\hat\mu_{kj}(\bmX_i)}{\pi_k(Z_i)}\bigg\}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-864: In the simulation, the observed baseline covariate vector is $\bmW=(X_c, X_b, Z_{\rm sub , Z_{\rm EW )$, where $X_c$ is a continuous covariate uniformly distributed over the interval $(-3,3)$, $X_b$ is a binary covariate with $P(X_b=1)=0.5$, $Z_{\rm sub}$ is a binary indicator for disease subtype with $P(Z_{\rm sub =1)=0.8$, and $Z_{\rm EW \in \{1,2,3\}$ denotes the enrollment window and satisfies \begin{equation}P(Z_{\rm EW}= t \mid X_c, X_b, Z_{\rm sub}, U) = \frac{\exp(Q_t)}{\exp(Q_1) + \exp(Q_2) + \exp(Q_3)}, \quad t=1,2,3,\end{equation} $Q_1 = 0.5 + X_c + 2X_b - Z_{\rm sub + U$, $Q_2 = 1 + 2X_c + X_b - Z_{\rm sub + U$, $Q_3 = -0.5 + X_c + X_b + Z_{\rm sub + U$, and the unobserved baseline variable $U$ follows a standard normal distribution and is independent of $X_c$, $X_b$, and $Z_{\rm sub}$.}}}}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-035: Derivation of Semiparametric Efficiency Lower Bound
elements.sent-2411-872: Suppose we are interested in comparing treatment effects between $j$ and $k$.. Note that $\pi_j(Z)=\pi_j(W)$.
elements.sent-2411-873: Namely, let $\pi_j(W)=p(j\mid W)$ and $R=r(W) = I(\pi_j(W)>0,\pi_k(W)>0)$ be a deterministic function of $W$.
elements.sent-2411-874: Given two arms $j$ and $k$, the following proof rely on a study of the statistical functional \begin{equation}\theta_{jk}=\Theta_{jk}(P)=E_P[Y^{(j)}\mid R=1]=E_P[\frac{I(A=j,R=1)Y}{\pi_j(W)}]/P(R=1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-875: For a given distribution $P_0\in {\cal M}$, denote the density of $P_0$ with respect to some dominating measure $\nu$ by $p_0$.
elements.sent-2411-876: For bounded $g\in L_2(P_0)$, we define the parametric submodel $p_t=(1+tg)p_0$, which is valid for small enough $t$ and has score $g$ for $t$ at $t=0$.
elements.sent-2411-877: Given that the model $\cal M$ is nonparametric and some $\phi^*_{jk}\in L_2(P_0)$ with $P_0\phi^*_{jk =0$, we will show that $\Theta_{jk (P_0)$ is pathwise differentiable with respect to $\cal M$ at $P_0$ with efficient influence function (EIF) $\phi^*_{jk}$ following from that for any $P_0\in\mathcal M$, we have \begin{equation}\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\Theta_{jk}(P_t)\big|_{t=0}=\langle \phi^*_{jk},g\rangle_{P_0},\end{equation} where $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{P_0}$ is the inner product in $L_2(P_0)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-878: The statistical functional can be written as \begin{equation}\theta_{jk,t}=\Theta_{jk}(P_t)=\int \frac{I(a=j,r(w)=1)y}{p_t(j\mid w)p_t(r(w)=1)}p_t(w,y,a)dwdyda,\end{equation} and we can see that \begin{equation}\Theta_{jk}(P_t)-\Theta_{jk}(P_0) =\int I(a=j,r=1)y\left\{\underbrace{\frac{p_t(w,y,a)}{p_t(j\mid w)p_t(R=1)}-\frac{p_t(w,y,a)}{p_0(j\mid w)p_t(R=1)}}_{u_1}\right. +\underbrace{\frac{p_t(w,y,a)}{p_0(j\mid w)p_t(R=1)}-\frac{p_t(w,y,a)}{p_0(j\mid w)p_0(R=1)}}_{u_2} +\left.\underbrace{\frac{p_t(w,y,a)}{p_0(j\mid w)p_0(R=1)}-\frac{p_0(w,y,a)}{p_0(j\mid w)p_0(R=1)}}_{u_3}\right\}dwdyda.\end{equation}. Analysis of $u_3$.
elements.sent-2411-879: It is straightforward that \begin{equation}u_3 = \int \frac{I(a=j,r=1)y}{p_0(j\mid w)p_0(R=1)}t p_0(w,y,a)g(w,y,a)dwdyda=t\langle \frac{I(a=j,r(w)=1)y}{p_0(j\mid w)p_0(R=1)}, g\rangle_{P_0}.\end{equation}. Analysis of $u_2$.
elements.sent-2411-880: Some calculations show that \begin{equation}p_t(R=1) =p_t(r(W)=1)=\int I(r(w)=1)p_t(w)dw =\int I(r(w)=1)p_t(w,y,a)dwdyda =p_0(R=1)+t\int I(r(w)=1)p_0(w,y,a)g(w,y,a)dwdyda.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-882: Therefore, it turns out that \begin{equation}u_2 =\int I(a=j,r=1)y\frac{p_0(w,y,a)+O(t)}{p_0(j\mid w)}(\frac{1}{p_t(R=1)}-\frac{1}{p_0(R=1)})dwdyda =\theta_{jk,0}p_0(R=1)(\frac{1}{p_t(R=1)}-\frac{1}{p_0(R=1)}) + o(t) =t\langle -\theta_{jk,0}\frac{I(r(w)=1)}{p_0(R=1)},g\rangle_{P_0} + o(t).\end{equation}. Analysis of $u_1$.
elements.sent-2411-883: Some calculations show that \begin{equation}\quad \frac{1}{p_t(j\mid w)}=\frac{p_t(w)}{p_t(a=j,w)}=\frac{\int p_t(w,y',a')dy'da'}{\int p_t(a=j,w,y')dy'} =\frac{p_0(w)+t\int p_0(w,y',a')g(w,y',a')dy'da'}{p_0(a=j,w)+t\int p_0(a=j,w,y')g(a=j,w,y')dy'} =\frac{p_0(w)}{p_0(a=j, w)}+t\frac{\int p_0(w,y',a')g(w,y',a')dy'da'}{p_0(a=j,w)}-t\frac{p_0(w)\int p_0(a=j,w,y')g(a=j,w,y')dy'}{p_0(a=j,w)^2}+o(t) =\frac{1}{p_0(j\mid w)}+t\frac{\int p_0(w,y',a')g(w,y',a')dy'da'}{p_0(a=j,w)}-t\frac{\int p_0(a=j,w,y')g(a=j,w,y')dy'}{p_0(j\mid w)p_0(a=j,w)}+o(t).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-884: We can rewrite $u_1$ as \begin{equation}u_1 =\int I(a=j,r=1)y(\frac{1}{p_t(j\mid w)}-\frac{1}{p_0(j\mid w)})(\frac{p_0(w,y,a)}{p_0(R=1)}+O(t))dwdyda =t\int\frac{I(a=j,r=1)y}{p_0(a=j,w)p_0(R=1)}p_0(w,y,a)dyda\int p_0(w,y',a')g(w,y',a')dy'da'dw \quad-t\int \frac{I(a=j,r=1)yp_0(w,y,a)}{p_0(j\mid w)p_0(a=j,w)p_0(R=1)}dyda\int p_0(a=j,w,y')g(a=j,w,y')dy'dw+o(t) =t\int\frac{I(r=1)yp_0(y\mid w,a=j)}{p_0(R=1)}dy\int p_0(w,y',a')g(w,y',a')dy'da'dw \quad-t\int \frac{I(r=1)yp_0(y\mid w,a=j)}{p_0(j\mid w)p_0(R=1)}dy\int p_0(a=j,w,y')g(a=j,w,y')dy'dw+o(t) =t\int\frac{I(r=1)}{p_0(R=1)}\mu_{jk}(w) p_0(w,y',a')g(w,y',a')dy'da'dw \quad-t\int \frac{I(r=1)}{p_0(j\mid w)p_0(R=1)}\mu_{jk}(w) p_0(a=j,w,y')g(a=j,w,y')dy'dw+o(t) =t\langle \frac{I(r=1)}{p_0(R=1)}(\mu_{jk}(w)-\frac{I(a=j)}{p_0(j\mid w)}\mu_{jk}(w)),g\rangle_{P_0}+o(t),\end{equation} where $\mu_{jk (w)=E[Y\mid W=w,A=j, r(W)=1]$ and we used that $\mu_{jk (w)=E[Y\mid W=w,A=j]$ under $I(r(W)=1)$.}}
elements.formula-2411-085: \begin{equation}u_1 =\int I(a=j,r=1)y(\frac{1}{p_t(j\mid w)}-\frac{1}{p_0(j\mid w)})(\frac{p_0(w,y,a)}{p_0(R=1)}+O(t))dwdyda =t\int\frac{I(a=j,r=1)y}{p_0(a=j,w)p_0(R=1)}p_0(w,y,a)dyda\int p_0(w,y',a')g(w,y',a')dy'da'dw \quad-t\int \frac{I(a=j,r=1)yp_0(w,y,a)}{p_0(j\mid w)p_0(a=j,w)p_0(R=1)}dyda\int p_0(a=j,w,y')g(a=j,w,y')dy'dw+o(t) =t\int\frac{I(r=1)yp_0(y\mid w,a=j)}{p_0(R=1)}dy\int p_0(w,y',a')g(w,y',a')dy'da'dw \quad-t\int \frac{I(r=1)yp_0(y\mid w,a=j)}{p_0(j\mid w)p_0(R=1)}dy\int p_0(a=j,w,y')g(a=j,w,y')dy'dw+o(t) =t\int\frac{I(r=1)}{p_0(R=1)}\mu_{jk}(w) p_0(w,y',a')g(w,y',a')dy'da'dw \quad-t\int \frac{I(r=1)}{p_0(j\mid w)p_0(R=1)}\mu_{jk}(w) p_0(a=j,w,y')g(a=j,w,y')dy'dw+o(t) =t\langle \frac{I(r=1)}{p_0(R=1)}(\mu_{jk}(w)-\frac{I(a=j)}{p_0(j\mid w)}\mu_{jk}(w)),g\rangle_{P_0}+o(t),\end{equation}
